If you move more than 60 ft away from the creatures location, then the fascination ends. 9 - Last Whisper When you kill a creature, you see their life flash before your eyes, learning one piece of information. * Greatest regret * Deepest secret * Closest companion 10 - Curtain Call Recharge Choose a direction and reduce your speed to 0 at the start of your turn. Until you move or are Poise broken you can only shoot in that direction (180 degrees), but you can increase the range of your pistol to 200. Once you need to reload, this effect ends.

entries.title: Concealment
entries.content: Concealment is a Circumstance that is gained when a creature's position is being obscured from another's Senses. Usually, this means an effect like fog or darkness is partially or totally hiding a creature from another, resulting in Partial Concealment or Total Concealment, but this may take other forms against creatures that have other senses. Concealment is a condition that's relative to the senses of other creatures. If you have Dark Vision and your opponent doesn't in a situation with Dark, you have concealment against them, but they don't have concealment to you. Attacking from concealment, even Total Concealment, does not Surprise by default. You still need to use +Stealth as normal.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Cover and Concealment/Concealment.md

entries.title: Cover
entries.content: Cover is an environmental Circumstance that increases your AC and Reflex by 2. Standard Cover is received against Ranged Attacks and AoEs that target you while there are obstacles between you and the source of the attack that obscure a large portion of your space, and that cannot be pierced by the attack. Better forms of cover result in Improved Cover and Total Cover. Examples include peeking around a pillar, over a trench, or behind a low wall.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Cover and Concealment/Cover.md

entries.title: Improved Cover
entries.content: Improved Cover is an environmental Circumstance that increases your AC and Reflex by 4. Improved Cover is received against Ranged Attacks and AoEs that target you while there are obstacles between you and the source of the attack that obscure almost all of your space, usually intentionally designed to allow attacks only in one direction, and that cannot be pierced by the attack. Examples include arrow slits and murder holes., as well as ranged weapon attacks against Submerged targets.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Cover and Concealment/Improved Cover.md

entries.title: Partial Concealment
entries.content: Partial Concealment is a Circumstance that decreases attacker's ACC by 2. When you have Partial Concealment, a creature can still see you, but in a limited capacity. Either they can only see your shadow, they're trying to avert your gaze, or there are poor environmental conditions/magic at play.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Cover and Concealment/Partial Concealment.md

entries.title: Total Concealment
entries.content: Total Concealment is a Circumstance that decreases attacker's ACC by 4. When you have Total Concealment, a creature cannot see you at all. Either they are Blinded, you are completely hidden in an environmental or magical effect, or you are Invisible.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Cover and Concealment/Total Concealment.md

entries.title: Total Cover
entries.content: Total Cover is an environmental Circumstance that makes you Untargetable. Total Cover is received against Ranged Attacks, AoEs, and Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Target Abilities that target you while there are obstacles between you and the source of the attack that leave no way to reach you, and that cannot be pierced by the attack (in the case of Ranged Attacks and Area Effects.)
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Cover and Concealment/Total Cover.md

entries.title: Abbreviations
entries.content: AC - Armor Class Attack - Accuracy AoE - Area of Effect CC - Debilitating Status AKA Crowd Control GM - Dungeon Master +HP - Hit Points LvL - Level NPC - Non-Player Character PC - Player Character CHA - Charisma DEX - Dexterity INT - Intelligence LUK - Luck STR - Strength WIS - Wisdom AoO - Attack of Opportunity Crit - Critical Hit DC - Difficulty Class AKA Target Number Dismissible - Dismissible
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Abbreviations.md

entries.title: AC
entries.content: Armor Class (AC) is the measure of how difficult it is to hit you. Targets must meet or exceed your AC with an Attack Roll to Damage you. By default, your starting AC is 10, and you add your LvL and Armor. DEX also gets added, but see Armor for how dex interacts with armor. It is also adjusted based on Circumstance.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/AC.md

entries.title: Advantage
entries.content: There are many abilities in the game that allow you additional odds success in some limited capacity. These are usually represented through rerolls and advantage. Rerolls do as they might suggest; they allow you to roll dice again after you didn't like the result. Whenever you use a reroll on a dice roll that you have advantage or disadvantage on, you make that reroll with advantage or disadvantage. Advantage allows you to roll 2 dice and take the desired of the 2 results. Inversely, disadvantage forces you to roll 2 dice and take the lower of the 2 results. Multiple instances of advantage allow you to roll the dice more, 3, 4, maybe even 5 times and take the desired result. Advantage and disadvantage cancel each other out. If that’s confusing, treat advantage as +1 and disadvantage as -1 in the chart below. | -3 | etc | | --- | -------------------------------- | | -2 | Roll three dice and take lowest | | -1 | Roll two dice and take lowest | | 0 | Roll 1 dice | | +1 | Roll two dice and take highest | | +2 | Roll three dice and take highest | | +3 | etc | When you take 10 on a skill when you have advantage on it, assuming it is a constant bonus (like from Skill Focus), you instead effectively take 15.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Advantage.md

entries.title: AoE
entries.content: Stands for "Area of Effect." It is used as shorthand to describe an attack that affects a wide area, such as an explosion, or a line of fire from a dragon's breath. Area attacks have a point of origin. When the origin is a creature, such as with a breath weapon, determine Cover from one specific point adjacent to it. If the origin is a targeted point in space, such as with an explosion radiating outward, that point should be used to determine cover. Some of the most common forms of AoE are radius, cone and line. Here are some diagrams and descriptions to help you understand these common examples. Radius Radius effects have you choose a point, often specifying if they can target a spot in midair or on the ground. The point determines the center of the radius, which then extends outwards in every direction x number of feet (usually 5-20). The initial space you targeted is also subject to this effect. A classic example of this is Fire Blast. Fire blast requires you to target a creature and has a 10 ft radius, so blasting a skeleton will look something like this, with the orange being the point you target. !601 Many radius effects have the user as the starting point of the radius. Most of these harmful effects are not intended to also effect the user. Some effects, like the Healer's Channel feature specifically mention that it only target's allies. Cone Cones also require you choose a point, almost always one adjacent to you, then the effect spreads out as it gets farther away from you. This can be easily figured out on a hex grid by just counting the number of hexes branching in three different directions (the solid lines) and then connecting the most distant points (the dotted lines). Cones can be relatively small, like with Frost Breath's 15 ft cone, or enormous, like with the Wildfire Wyrm's 60 ft cone breath weapon. !632 Line Line effects are a bit more complex. They also tend to originate from the caster, with some notable exceptions being some persistent effects like Wall of Fire, or ones that move around like Temperamental Tempest. In any case, lines all follow the same targeting logic. They can be very versatile, allowing you to precisely target creatures in a chaotic battlefield of friend and foe. The general idea is to have a general pattern you want to adhere to. A simple pattern is easily recognizable as a line. Lets say you have a 40 ft line attack. !410 Unfortunately, with a hex/square grid, getting some types of lines are hard to replicate with these simple lines. By "pattern" I mean when you want to have the line "jump" diagonally slightly. This could mean slightly adjusting the angle every 10, 15 or 30 feet. so you could hit both skeletons by making a line that jumps every 20 ft like this. !475 Let's make things a little more complicated. You got Knocked Down so you cant move and your pesky teammate is in the way. Let's try again. In such a scenario, this could be a legal line, but you're only hitting one guy. !386 This would also be legal...But your ally might not be too happy about it. Notice how the line moves in regular 10 ft intervals before each diagonal "jump." !384 This however, would be an illegal line, even if it would maximize your damage and avoid hitting your teammate. It's two different lines, not one. Only rare spells let you decide the shape of your line like Fire Snake. !335
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/AoE.md

entries.title: Attack
entries.content: What you add to your Attack Rolls to beat a target's AC. By default, you add your LV, DEX or STR depending on your weapon type, and bonuses from your +Class to your accuracy. Circumstance can also change acc.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Attack.md

entries.title: CC
entries.content: A Debilitating Status (CC) is one that impedes your movement or prevents you from taking actions normally.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/CC.md

entries.title: Circumstance
entries.content: Circumstance is any in-game situation, event, or position that conditionally applies a bonus, penalty, or Status to a creature. Circumstances can apply to different creatures in different ways, usually based on positioning or the presence or absence of abilities - for example, having a low wall to grant you Cover will work for any creatures on the other side of the wall, but not on the same side as you. One creature may see you while you're in a cloud, while another cannot. You may be above one creature, but another creature may be above you. Therefore, these effects are circumstantial.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Circumstance.md

entries.title: Crust
entries.content: Crusty Content is content that I believe is still too early in development to be worth your time. It will often be incomplete and imbalanced.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Crust.md

entries.title: DC
entries.content: Difficulty Class (DC) is the target number that a Roll must reach to be considered a success.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/DC.md

entries.title: Dispel
entries.content: Dispel refers to when a magical effect is negated, usually functioning as Dispel Magic. Other effects, such as techniques in the Arc tree or magic crash can also perform a similar function.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Dispel.md

entries.title: DR
entries.content: Damage Reduction (DR) is a rare form of defense that reduces incoming damage by a flat amount, noted as a value (DR x). DR applies to slash, pierce, crush and Poise damage, and DRA applies to all Damage types (minus True Damage). All DR stacks. Stacking defenses works in the way that protects the most - such as applying DR after Resistance. If an effect's damage is reduced to 0 with DR, then any associated non-damaging effects are negated. DR cannot block damage from the same attack twice, such as from a flaming sword, or an attack that deals poise damage and hp damage.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/DR.md

entries.title: Flanking
entries.content: Flanking is a Circumstance that grants +2 Attack against a target that you and an ally are Threatening from opposite sides.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Flanking.md

entries.title: Forced Movement
entries.content: Forced movement refers to any movement that does not happen willingly. This could mean you getting affected by a spell, the stairs you're climbing suddenly turning into a steep incline from a trap etc. By default, forced movement does not provoke AoOs. Many exceptions are made on a case by case basis, such as when a flying creatures is Grounded and falls from the sky, or when you shove a creature with the Maneuver Master feat.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Forced Movement.md

entries.title: FRA
entries.content: Full-Round Action
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/FRA.md

entries.title: GM Magic
entries.content: Sometimes I will refer to things by this term. It's a vague term meant to apply to any rules your GM makes up that aren't part of the original rules. This could mean a special type of ritual to solve a very particular problem, release Fiends into the Mortal Realm or perform some other kind of incredible feat. Or it can be some type of special ability your GM gave you as a quest reward. Basically, anything your GM makes up that can have major gameplay implications.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/GM Magic.md

entries.title: GM
entries.content: Game Master. The person running the game. This person is the arbiter of the rules, tasked with creating the world, playing most (if not all) NPCs, and ensuring a fun and fair experience for the players.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/GM.md

entries.title: Handedness
entries.content: What is in your hands is very important in Nick Nacks. Casting spells requires a spell focus, which requires at least one hand. Weapons, shields, Dual Wielding etc all require hands. Swap Item is the core action that you change what occupies your hands, which is usually done at start of turn. Changing Handedness Mid-Turn Some abilities function differently depending on what is in your hands. A common example is the damage scaling component for keeping your hands free in Shapeshifter, Fiend Shifter and Martial Artist. These classes require you have 2 free hands in order to maximize the damage from the class specific attack forms. If, for example, you disarm a creature and take its weapon, this updates the damage these attacks deal immediately (to deal less damage). This also functions in reverse, allowing you to benefit from this enhanced damage if you were previously holding something and choose to drop it.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Handedness.md

entries.title: Helpless
entries.content: Helpless is a Circumstance like being tied up with rope, being Asleep, in Time Out or being Defeated. This makes the creature unable to resists a wide variety of effects. Many effects only function against defeated or willing creatures. Helpless creatures are considered defeated for the purposes of such effects.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Helpless.md

entries.title: Initiative
entries.content: Nick Nacks is a turn-based game, and initiative decides who goes first One of the players rolls a d10. On a 6 or higher, the players go first, or a 5 or lower, the enemies go first. On the first round of combat, whichever party went first decides which creature acts first. Then you swap to the other party and they decide who goes. You repeat this process until either every creature has acted, or one side has several creatures that haven't acted. All remaining creatures act on the same turn and then you start back from the top, using the same order as round 1. When initiative is rolled, all Buff effects disappear automatically (unless its duration is longer than 1 minute). This is to discourage spamming buffing spells and then waiting to recover mana. Surprise In DnD and its derivatives, if you get the jump on your opponent, the sneaky party gets an entire turn to themselves. This is not the case in Nick Nacks. The conventional "surprise round" is now a normal round in combat. All characters that are surprised lose are DD during the first round, and the surprising party always goes first in initiative. Surprise can be a bit finicky sometimes, making you question if only some characters should be surprised. If that's what your gut tells you, that's how it is. For example, your party's rogue who was hiding in an alley ambushes a group of guardsmen that are harassing the rest of the party. The guard captain notices but his men do not. Captain is not DD, the rest are. What happens when our party starts fighting in the middle of talking? In the event where there are two parties that don't immediately fight, when initiative starts is usually determined by whenever someone takes an aggressive action, such as casting a spell or drawing an arrow. In these cases, where both parties are aware of each other, initiative gets rolled immediately before any actions happen. Certain types of underhanded tactics can get around this, such as using sleight of hand to draw concealed weapons or deception for a sucker punch. See surprise for more details.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Initiative.md

entries.title: Luck Dice
entries.content: A luck dice is a meta resource given to characters that allows them to bend fate in their favor, awarded by various things throughout the game, namely for the Lunatic. You can spend a luck dice whenever you don't like the result of a d20 roll you made. You can even do it retroactively after you discover your roll failed.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Luck Dice.md

entries.title: LvL
entries.content: The level assigned to a creature or effect is used to represent their relative strength in the setting. Level is added to all Rolls (Attack Rolls, +Saving Throws, proficient Skills, etc.)
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/LvL.md

entries.title: Magical Specialization
entries.content: Characters with magical specialization start play with 6 +Cantrips. They also get points in the Academy technique tree equal to how many technique points they have in their highest tier magical technique tree.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Magical Specialization.md

entries.title: Mana Reserves
entries.content: Mana reserves function exactly like your base mana, except they disappear after used, and only regenerate after you rest. Mana reserves are gained through having positive intelligence, are sometimes given to mage classes and through magic items, often with particular restrictions on their use.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Mana Reserves.md

entries.title: Mana
entries.content: If you are interested in the crafting material, see crystallized mana. Mana is the resource that enables all sorts of things. For the purposes of this explanation, mana is primary used to fuel techniques. It also fuels mana class abilities and is a crafting component. Mana itself has value. Mana Basics In order to use a technique, you must spend mana equal to the tier of technique. Cantrips have an effective tier of 0 and do not cost mana. While not in immediate danger (such as combat or a chase), you regenerate 1 mana per turn, or 10 mana per minute. | LV | Mana | Tier/Cost | Technique Cap | | --- | ---- | --------- | ------------- | | 1 | 4 | 1 | 2 | | 2 | 6 | | | | 3 | 8 | 2 | 3 | | 4 | 10 | | | | 5 | 12 | 3 | 4 | | 6 | 14 | | | | 7 | 16 | 4 | 5 | | 8 | 18 | | | | 9 | 20 | 5 | 6 | | 10 | 22 | | | | 11 | 24 | 6 | 7 | | 12 | 26 | | | See Mana Burn for how effects can reduce your mana. Technique Cap Techniques have many different action types, and so that means that you could theoretically have extremely powerful turns using high level buffs as a swift action, powerful special attacks and free action techniques like Focus Punch. To prevent this, a cap is introduced. The cap is (your highest level technique+1). There is not a cap on how much mana you can spend in a turn. Go wild with class features, magic items and metamagic feats in the +Magic Feats section.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Mana.md

entries.title: Martial Specialization
entries.content: Characters with martiaa specialization get points in the Warfare technique tree equal to how many technique points they have in another magical technique tree.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Martial Specialization.md

entries.title: Memory
entries.content: Memory determines how many Techniques you can have at your disposal at once. At the start of each day, you can prepare a number of techniques equal to your memory, chosen from any technique trees you have technique points invested into.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Memory.md

entries.title: Minion
entries.content: Minion is a Circumstantial label assigned to a creature as it relates to another. A creature that is 1/3 of the level of another or lower is a Minion compared to it. A level 0 creature is always considered a Minion to any creature of a higher level.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Minion.md

entries.title: Natural
entries.content: A Natural result on a Roll is the result without any bonuses or penalties.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Natural.md

entries.title: NPC
entries.content: Non-Player Character (NPC): Any character that is played by the GM. These characters usually get placed after PCs to favor the players.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/NPC.md

entries.title: On-hit
entries.content: On-hit effects only happen when you successfully match or exceed a creature's AC and deal damage to a creature (AKA it is not negated by DR/Resistance). The effect does not occur on a graze. When a creature has an on-hit effect that has a saving throw attached to it like the Grounding, weapon quality, the creature makes the save only once if they are hit multiple times. Each application beyond the first applies a -2 penalty to the save.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/On-hit.md

entries.title: PC
entries.content: Player Character (PC): A character controlled by a player, and not the GM. Players are the main characters of a story, and thus gain priority in some aspects. Sometimes, a DM will play a special character alongside the players that is treated like a PC. This is normal, but the players can veto such a character's PC status if they feel it is inappropriate.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/PC.md

entries.title: Permanent Status
entries.content: Permanent refers to status that is not alleviated on its own, and takes time or specific measures to remove. Many Healing or dispel effects cannot remove permanent status. Includes, but is not limited to, the following... Exhausted Drained Cursed Affliction Mana Burn
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Permanent Status.md

entries.title: Poise
entries.content: +Combat Maneuvers are forms of attack that do not directly deal damage and are dependent on a stat called poise. Poise determines how difficult you are to toss around. It functions similarly to hp, but you subtract the attack roll from the target's poise. If this reduces them to 0 poise, they suffer the effects of the maneuver and their poise resets to maximum. This is referred to as "poise breaking." Poise naturally resets after combat ends or by taking the total defense action. Other effects that toss characters around might also interact with poise. If poise is reduced via lowered stats without a combat maneuver being used, like with Power Pose, it drops to a minimum of 1 (unless otherwise specified). - Using a combat maneuver replaces melee attacks by default. - Any damage reduction also applies to poise damage. - Combat maneuvers by default have the Martial tag, allowing you to use the attack, damage and bonus effects of a held weapon or natural attack, even if it states you use another attack form. - Poise damage counts as "taking damage" for all effects that refer to such, unless the effect specifies +HP damage. Poise is calculated as follows. Common things like armor, shields and feats add to poise. All bonuses to poise stack. - 10 + 2xLvL + STR + DEX + Armor/shields + class + etc
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Poise.md

entries.title: Proc
entries.content: Proc is a terminology I have only really heard in the context of games. It means that something is triggered. For instance, when I land an attack with my bleed katana, I would say that I "procced" the bleed.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Proc.md

entries.title: Random Direction
entries.content: Some effects in the game launch creatures, but do so in unpredictable ways, like Temperamental Tempest and Rift Bolt. To simplify things, I will present a unified method to help you determine what that direction is. Because the direction isn't taking into account terrain, it could very well move a creature directly into a wall. All directions are valid. Nick Nacks is expected to take place on a grid, either with hexes or squares. As such there are already a limited number of directions. For hexes that is 6, for squares that is 8. For hexes, roll a d6. For squares, roll a d8. Rolls of 1 should be directly up, with each space moving clockwise being 1 higher. Since most of the time horizontal movement is all that matters, directions pushing up or down should not be considered unless you're feeling wild. Here are some diagrams to help visualize. !Hexes|455 !Squares
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Random Direction.md

entries.title: Reach
entries.content: Reach determines how far a creature can make melee attacks from. Some features and equipment, like using a weapon with the reach property, increases your reach for attacks, but it does not increase your reach for other features. Natural reach determines what you character can reach without the aid of any magic or extraordinary feature. You can interact with objects within your natural reach. Any attacks you make without added features are assumed to have this natural reach. See Size for how it affects your reach.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Reach.md

entries.title: Roll
entries.content: The default die roll for resolving things. This is used to determine the variance expected. In Nick Nacks, this is a 20 sided dice for most rolls. Other dice are typically only used in damage rolls and other niche cases.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Roll.md

entries.title: Sp
entries.content: Spell abilities encompass any ability that is magical that is a Spell. Sometimes used in notation for abilities that mimic or function like spells.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Sp.md

entries.title: Su
entries.content: Supernatural abilities encompass any ability that is magical, but not a Spell. Many supernatural abilities are persistent effects on a creature. If such a thing is ever dispelled, then if must be reactivated as a swift action.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Su.md

entries.title: Technique Point
entries.content: A technique point gives you access to the next tier of a technique tree equal to the number of points you have invested in it, from 1 to 6. Most classes get technique points on odd levels, and all player characters get technique points on even levels. You are capped at how many technique points you can put into a tree by your LvL. Level 1 characters can only have access to tier 1 tech, level 3 get tier 2 and so on, as presented in graph below. | Technique Level (and points required) | Character Level | | ------------------------------------- | --------------- | | 1 | 1 | | 2 | 3 | | 3 | 5 | | 4 | 7 | | 5 | 9 | | 6 | 11 |
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Technique Point.md

entries.title: Threaten
entries.content: The area a creature threatens represents where they can make attacks. This usually only applies to melee attacks. This area also marks where it is typically not safe to perform certain actions, or risk getting hit with an AoO.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/Threaten.md

entries.title: True Damage
entries.content: True damage cannot be reduced resistance, DR and similar effects that reduce damage. The most common source of true damage is Bleeding
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/True Damage.md

entries.title: XP
entries.content: Crusty content Experience is a meta-currency gained through prioritizing primary campaign events that allows players to influence the story/chronological order of things in order to accomplish personal goals quickly and get back into the action faster. As a baseline, 10 XP is the equivalent of one in-game day of events. Experience is rewarded for spending time working towards campaign goals in an active manner. This mainly means exploring dungeons, splitting heads, and overthrowing governments - anything that is both related to the main campaign objectives, and is not part of downtime events (such as recovering from an Affliction or Crafting). XP is rewarded based on the time required to complete the mission, with additional bonuses for overcoming particular challenges. The purpose of XP is to reward taking action. While downtime is an important part of roleplay, it's easy to justify staying in town making safe money or doing side quests to save up for a shiny new weapon or achieve personal goals if there isn't an obvious time constraint on important story events. It's easy to hold off on an important event and try to squeeze a few more days of crafting into your schedule. Instead, XP serves to reward taking action immediately, without penalizing downtime events like crafting.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Abbreviations and Terminology/XP.md

entries.title: Ability Check
entries.content: An ability check is something that can reasonably be covered by an ability score, but cannot be associated with any particular skill. For example if you forget something important out of character, or if out of character you aren't thinking about something, the GM might have you roll an INT check to see if your character recalls it. In such scenarios, you roll and add the relevant ability score and anything that can be added to ability checks. Luck Check is its own separate thing that has other factors that can influence it.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Ability Scores/Ability Check.md

entries.title: Ability Scores
entries.content: These are representations of a creature's innate or cultivated affinities. These represent the mind, body, and spirit. The ability scores are Strength, Dexterity, Luck, Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma. Every Player Character gets 6 points to increase their ability scores as they wish, starting from 0 in each. You can decrease a ability scores to -1 in order to gain an additional point. The maximum points you can invest in a given ability score is 3. !Ability Scores.png
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Ability Scores/Ability Scores.md

entries.title: Carry Weight
entries.content: A creature's Carry Weight represents what it is capable of lifting and moving around with without much effort. The default value is 100. A creature can lift and drag twice as much as their carry weight, but is staggered while doing so. | Bonuses to Carry Weight | | | ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | Strength | +100 for each point, additive (or -50 for negative str) | | Size | Quadruple Carry Weight (and weight) for each size above medium. (or 1/2 Carry Weight and 1/4 weight for each size below medium) |
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Ability Scores/Carry Weight.md

entries.title: CHA
entries.content: Your leadership and personal magnetism. - Positive charisma enhances your Companion creatures. Any creature that takes a companion slot gains your CHA to their Attack (unless you are Defeated) - With positive charisma, you get a teamwork feat - For every point of CHA, you can grant another creature one of your teamwork feats. To do this, you must spend 1 hour doing training exercises with the creatures you intend to team up with. (allies can't use while you are defeated) * - You lose a companion slot with negative CHA. - Negative CHA reduces your companion's Accuracy - You cannot benefit from teamwork feats with negative charisma
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Ability Scores/CHA.md

entries.title: DEX
entries.content: Your agility, hand-eye coordination, and general sneakiness. - You add DEX to your Reflex Save. - Depending on your choice in armor, you can add your dexterity to your AC, but you always add it to Poise - You add dexterity to Attack and Damage with ranged weapons and light weapons (but not at the same time as strength) * - Negative DEX subtracts from these values.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Ability Scores/DEX.md

entries.title: Extraordinary Strength
entries.content: There are numerous ways to increase your ability to lift and break things in the game. In order to make that investment seem valuable and to maintain thematic cohesion, many abilities that allow you to throw your opponent easily have this "extraordinary strength clause." Without it, you might get fairies able to toss around giants. I know that not all creatures of the same size are the same weight, but this is an abstraction to get a sense of where, ballpark, you should be in order to start thinking about flipping giant monsters like they are pancakes. | Creature Size | Carry Weight | STR Bypass | | --------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | | Medium | ~100 | +0 | | Large | ~400 | +3 | | Huge | ~1,600 | +4 | | Massive | ~6,400+ | +4 | Basically, if your STR or carryweight matches or exceeds these numbers, go wild. If not, then tossing creatures shouldn't be something you can do. Common ways to boost carryweight outside of STR... - Increasing your Size - Indulging in cataclysm techniques or deific boons from Caldera, the Cataclysm - Race features (like Amazon's Titanic Lift)
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Ability Scores/Extraordinary Strength.md

entries.title: INT
entries.content: Your knowledge, magical expertise, and logic capacity. - You gain an additional point of Memory per point of INT - You gain an additional Skill Proficiency per point of INT - You gain two additional Languages per point of INT - You gain 1 Mana for each point in intelligence - You add your INT to you Caster Level * - Negative int subtracts from these values. In particular, you lose max mana equal to your negative int. Intelligence and Sentience A creature is considered sentient if it has at least -2 intelligence. These creatures typically do not speak, but can understand at least 1 language. Animal intelligence is -3 intelligence. Creatures with animal intelligence only understand simple commands (if anything). They are usually limited to their instinctual urges. Mindless creatures possess no will of their own. They act completely on instinct.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Ability Scores/INT.md

entries.title: Luck Check
entries.content: Luck checks are dice rolls that only add the creatures LUK stat. Separate from Serendipity checks, which are a more specific type of luck check.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Ability Scores/Luck Check.md

entries.title: LUK
entries.content: Your ability to turn tides and benefit from unlikely outcomes. - You get a number of Boons equal to your LUK score - You add LUK to Luck Checks. - You can add LUK to a Roll as an Immediate Action (that did not already add LUK). You must roll to add luck, so you cannot add it when you take 10 (such as when using skills for income) or take 20 - Negative LUK give you a bane per point of negative luck - Your DC decreases by your negative luck - Subtracted from luck checks
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Ability Scores/LUK.md

entries.title: Prayer
entries.content: You get a number of uses of prayer equal to (WIS+1). The default use of prayer is praying. Praying requires 1 minute of uninterrupted concentration. You gain impressions from the future about something in specific. When you initially start praying, you must mention a specific action (attack the bandits, pet the dog etc.). When the prayer is complete, gain a nugget information about whether this action will bring favorable or unfavorable results to you within the next half hour. The answer does not take into account consequences into the future past 30 minutes. | Weal | The action will give mostly good results | | ------------ | ------------------------------------------------------ | | Woe | The action will give mostly bad results | | Weal and Woe | The action will give good and bad results | | Nothing | The action won't have particularly good or bad results |
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Ability Scores/Prayer.md

entries.title: Serendipity
entries.content: When playing table top, a lot of things aren't well defined until they get into render distance, which basically means when players ask about it. When a situation pops up where your player asks for a specific circumstance like "Is the door to the armory locked?" "is there a hat store in town?" or "are there any footholds on the side of this cliff?" and you don't immediately have a response, the character in question rolls a luck check. If it's a general party question, or if you bring your lucky friend with you during your shopping trip, they could reasonably roll it instead. | Likelihood | DC | Description | | ------------------ | --- | ------------------------------------------------ | | Likely | 5 | "Are there footholds on the side of this cliff?" | | Plausible | 10 | "Is there a hat store in town?" | | Unlikely | 15 | "Is the door to this person's house unlocked?" | | Very unlikely | 20 | "Any human meat for sale?" | | Usually Impossible | 25 | "There any lava pools in this forest?" | Again, if you already had an answer in mind, don't roll this. This is something that you should use in moderation. The moment you let your players start rolling this again and again is when all cohesion in your world evaporates
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Ability Scores/Serendipity.md

entries.title: STR
entries.content: Your fortitude, athleticism, and endurance. - You add STR to your Fortitude Save. - You add STR to your Poise - You add +100 Carry Weight for each point of strength - Add +1 inventory slot per point of str - Every point of strength increases your Jump distance by 5 ft. * - Negative STR subtracts from your Fortitude Saves. - Negative STR halves your Carry Weight. - Subtract -1 inventory slot per negative point of str
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Ability Scores/STR.md

entries.title: WIS
entries.content: Your faith, decision making, habits, diligence, and discipline. - You add WIS to your Will Save. - You add your wisdom to +Perception, the skill all creatures get for free - You can pray to your god for guidance (see below for details). You can use prayer a number of times per day equal to your wisdom bonus. You can also use it for Invoking Deities. - Positive wisdom increases GBP gains, giving you deific boons boons earlier and in higher quantities * - Negative WIS subtracts from these values - Negative wisdom represents a complete lack of faith in most gods or being rejected by them, you cannot gain GBP (unless in service of Frogg Saran)
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Ability Scores/WIS.md

entries.title: Burrow
entries.content: Burrow Speeds allow Movement through dirt, clay, and other particulate turf, but not solid rocks (unless otherwise stated). Burrowing leaves a temporary hole the size of the creature that burrowed for a few rounds, depending on the sediment type, unless the creature takes measures to avoid this. Starting to burrow is a Swift Action that Provokes as if leaving a Threatened space.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Movement/Burrow.md

entries.title: Climb
entries.content: A creature with a climb speed can easily ascend or descend trees, walls buildings etc, provided there are ample places to place hands, feet etc. Creatures with climb speeds can even climb things without ample handholds using creative methods or exceptional biology in some cases (gecko, snake wrapping around).
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Movement/Climb.md

entries.title: Difficult Terrain
entries.content: Difficult Terrain is a Circumstance that represents reduced movement due to physical blockage. This can be because of wading through knee-deep mud, flying through dense brush, or burrowing through particularly rocky turf.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Movement/Difficult Terrain.md

entries.title: Fly
entries.content: Fly Speeds allow Movement in any direction, treating spaces moving directly into the air as Difficult Terrain. - A creature that is Entangled or Grounded cannot continue flight. - A creature that takes +HP damage from an Attack Roll or AoE effect while flying receives Clumsy 1 until they land, at which point the clumsy is removed. - Starting to fly is a Swift Action that Provokes as if leaving a Threatened space. Some mobility effects that place you into the air like Sky Uppercut or Extreme Speed can reasonably bypass this action requirement.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Movement/Fly.md

entries.title: Glide
entries.content: While not movement impaired, take no fall damage. When falling, move horizontally 5 feet per 1 foot fallen at a speed of 60.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Movement/Glide.md

entries.title: Hover
entries.content: Hover is a lesser version of Speeds allow Movement above solid and liquid surfaces while ignoring Difficult Terrain. A creature that is Grounded cannot hover.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Movement/Hover.md

entries.title: Land Speed
entries.content: Land speed refers to the generic speed most creatures use to walk around. Default land speed is 30 ft. race, +Class, Equipment, and Status can change this. Some actions grant extra speed. You may have other forms of movement that manifest in terms of different Speeds - you may use multiple forms of movement on the same turn, but only up to the maximum amount for the given speed. As an example, if you have a Fly 40 and a land speed of 30, you may walk 30 feet and then fly another 10.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Movement/Land Speed.md

entries.title: Movement
entries.content: You can move up to your Land Speed on your turn. Some abilities or effects may require spending a certain amount of your movement for the turn. Abilities that move you do not cost or consume any of your movement unless otherwise stated.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Movement/Movement.md

entries.title: Strafing
entries.content: Strafing is any movement in a threatened space that is not a direct path closer to the target. Usually this means moving to get into Flanking, or trying to position yourself in a better spot to Shove for instance. Strafing Provokes just like exiting threatened spaces. Dev Note: The purpose of this rule is to get melee characters leeway when moving up to creatures, but discouraging free movement around someone. It would be pretty silly if you could move 10,000 feet in circles around someone, but then step away from them and only THEN could they attack you.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Movement/Strafing.md

entries.title: Swim
entries.content: Creatures with a swim speed take no penalties to fighting underwater. Most creatures with a swim speed are either aquatic or Amphibious, allowing them to comfortably exist underwater for long periods of time or indefinitely. Proficiency in +Athletics is not the same as having a swim speed.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Movement/Swim.md

entries.title: Action System
entries.content: This game is played turn-based, with turn order determined at the start of the battle with an Initiative roll. The actions you can take on your turn are as follows. - Standard Action: This action occupies most of your turn, and you can perform one per turn. - Swift Action: This action can be executed during any other action, with a limit of one per turn. - Free Action: These actions are effortless or part of other actions, allowing you to perform as many as you wish per turn - within reason. - Full-Round Action: This action requires the use of your standard action and all of your Movement. You may still move if you have an ability that allows extra movement as a different action. - Movement: You can move up to your Speed on your turn. Some abilities or effects may require spending a certain amount of your movement for the turn. You may have other forms of movement that manifest in terms of different Speeds - you may use multiple forms of movement on the same turn, but only up to the maximum amount for the given speed. Any slower speeds used overlap - That is, if you have a Fly 40 and a ground speed of 30, you may walk 30 feet and then fly another 10. When it's not your turn, you can take the following actions: - Immediate Action: A Swift Action that can occur off-turn. Limited to one per round, shared with Swift Action — you cannot use both in the same round. - Instant Action: A Free Action that can occur off-turn. Referred to as "Instantly" in abilities. - Attack of Opportunity: A response triggered by certain actions of others. Limited to one per round. When a creature "Provokes," they are engaging in an action that allows others to AoO them.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Action System.md

entries.title: AoO
entries.content: Short for "Attack of Opportunity" An AoO is an Instant Action Attack Roll triggered in response to certain actions of others. All creatures can do this once per round by default. When a creature "Provokes," they are engaging in an action that allows others to AoO them. An AoO always happens before the triggering action. Movement can only provoke once from a given creature per turn. Creatures also threaten all spaces they occupy by default. You can enter the space of a creature that is a size category larger or smaller than you. If you enter or leave the spaces occupied by a creature, it Provokes an AoO as though you were leaving a threatened area. If you get attacked this way, the same creature can't also AoO you for leaving their threatened square in the same turn.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/AoO.md

entries.title: Free Action
entries.content: - Free Action: These actions are effortless or part of other actions, allowing you to perform as many as you wish per turn - within reason. See: Delay Swap Item +Monster Lore
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Free Action.md

entries.title: Full-Round Action
entries.content: - Full-Round Action: This action requires the use of your standard action and all of your movement. You may still move if you have an ability that allows extra movement as a different action.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Full-Round Action.md

entries.title: Immediate Action
entries.content: A Swift Action that can occur off-turn. Limited to one per round, shared with Swift Action — you cannot use both in the same round. You cannot use an immediate action AND an instant action in response to the same trigger, ie taking the Block action for you and an ally against a Kazekenshin Steel Tempest.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Immediate Action.md

entries.title: Instant Action
entries.content: A Free Action that can occur off-turn. You cannot use an immediate action AND an instant action, or multiple instant actions, in response to the same trigger, ie taking the Block action for you and an ally against a cleave.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Instant Action.md

entries.title: Standard Action
entries.content: - Standard Action: This action occupies most of your turn, and you can perform one per turn. You can also use your standard action to perform a swift action. - Example: The Attack Action is the most common, allowing a series of smaller attacks up to your Attack Speed, executed in any order. Refer to the "Attack" section for more details. See: Administer Potion Aid Carry Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Full Round Actions/Charge Dash Disengage Prepare Total Defense
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Standard Action.md

entries.title: Swift Action
entries.content: - Swift Action: This action can be executed during any other action, with a limit of one per turn. See: Hide Enhanced Jump Use Potion Recharge Abilities
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Action System/Swift Action.md

entries.title: Delay
entries.content: You can decide to wait to act in initiative, acting whenever you like at the end of another creature's turn, permanently changing your position in initiative.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Free Actions/Delay.md

entries.title: Swap Item
entries.content: At the start of your turn, you can decide what's in your hands from your inventory. You can draw and put away any weapons without penalty. This is a free action as long as it's done at the start of your turn. If you decide to change what you're wielding at another point in your turn, it becomes a swift action - and you can't do both.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Free Actions/Swap Item.md

entries.title: Charge
entries.content: Move at twice your speed in a straight line, halting when your foe is adjacent, or at least within reach. Make your attack(s) against a creature (including a Multiattack). Charging is reckless, so creatures get a +2 bonus to attack against you until the start of your next turn. If your charge gets interrupted for whatever reason, you must make your attack(s) from your halted position. You cannot charge a creature that is already adjacent to you.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Full Round Actions/Charge.md

entries.title: Administer Potion
entries.content: Give a potion to an unconscious ally. You can use potions from either of your inventories. A conscious ally must spend an Immediate Action to drink an administered potion.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Standard Actions/Administer Potion.md

entries.title: Aid
entries.content: Provide one of the following bonuses to an ally within your reach for 1 round (up to 3 times): +2 to Attack +2 to Saves +2 to AC Alternatively, remove one stack of the following conditions: Clumsy, Shaken, Sickened. It can also remove the charmed or Knocked Down conditions. You can also use aid to breakout any ally trapped by objects. You can aid a defeated creature when not in combat, bringing them to 1 hp. Extreme circumstances may require more assistance to restore a creature particularly out of it (such as medicine or magic).
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Standard Actions/Aid.md

entries.title: Carry
entries.content: Carrying an ally allows you to move them with your movement. You must be able to lift the ally. If you can't, you drag them, treating all terrain as difficult terrain. You must use this action every turn you carry an ally. The creature you carry becomes clumsy 2 unless they are defeated or you aren't in combat.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Standard Actions/Carry.md

entries.title: Dash
entries.content: Double your speed this round. Has no effect if your speed is zero.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Standard Actions/Dash.md

entries.title: Disengage
entries.content: Your movement is Elusive this turn.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Standard Actions/Disengage.md

entries.title: Focus
entries.content: Choose a creature you can see. For 1 round you gain the following benefits... - Your movement is elusive to them - You can take an immediate action as an instant action 1/turn in response to their actions. - You cannot be denied instant actions and immediate actions against the focused creature, even while DD. While you focus on a creature, you pay less attention to all other creatures, and are automatically DD to them. You can choose to continue or end focus on a creature on subsequent turns as a free action. Special - In organized duels, it is assumed both characters start the combat in this state. In such circumstances, they ignore the "you are DD to others" portion of this action (for companions and such).
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Standard Actions/Focus.md

entries.title: Prepare
entries.content: You can prepare swift actions, movement, free actions and standard actions to react to a specific triggering event (Ex: “a goblin opens this door, I walk up to him and shove him off the cliff”). If the triggering action never happens, you don't get your actions back. When you prepare a standard action to attack, you can only prepare a single attack in your Multiattack. You cannot prepare actions outside of Initiative.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Standard Actions/Prepare.md

entries.title: Total Defense
entries.content: Get DRA 2 until the start of your next turn. Remove all clumsy stacks and reset your poise to maximum. You can't make an AoO while using Total Defense.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Standard Actions/Total Defense.md

entries.title: Get Down!
entries.content: Throw yourself into the way of an attack made against a helpless creature. This could mean a minion, someone who is asleep or defeated. You can choose to become the target of all danger aimed at them, including AoE, spells and attacks. Such attacks automatically hit you and you get no saving throws against spells. You naturally need to be aware of an attack to protect against it. If a creature manages to give you the slip with stealth, then you can't protect. If the attack was originally a crit, it crits you instead automatically. AoE effects harm you twice.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Swift and Immediate Actions/Get Down!.md

entries.title: Jump
entries.content: Jump in a straight line. This can only be used in combination with other forms of movement such as walking or charging. Jump is also a static distance. By default, your Jump is 5. This is a measure of distance, specifically for horizontal jumps, while vertical jumps are half. This does not give you additional movement, and thus it is limited by your Land Speed. It can be used to avoid Difficult Terrain, but not if you're already in the Difficult Terrain. You can only jump once with this action. If you Dash, you can jump twice as part of the same jump action. These jumps do not need to be consecutive. Jump distance increases by 5 ft per point of STR (and DEX with +Acrobatics).
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Swift and Immediate Actions/Jump.md

entries.title: Mount
entries.content: Hop onto a willing creature's back, using their movement instead of your own. If you start combat mounted, your position in Initiative matches theirs. You can also dismount as a immediate action. You subtract whatever movement your mount used on their turn from your own for the round you dismount. If you are inflicted with any form of Forced Movement that knocks you more than 5 ft away from your mount, you are automatically dismounted. If your mount is knocked more than 5 ft away from you, you can choose to either hold on tight or hop off (requiring an immediate action). If your mount is Knocked Down or poise broken, you are set to 1 poise.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Swift and Immediate Actions/Mount.md

entries.title: Use Potion
entries.content: Retrieve and drink a potion. Provokes AoOs.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Swift and Immediate Actions/Use Potion.md

entries.title: Alternative Win Conditions
entries.content: For when killing everything that moves isn't what you want, alternative win conditions seek to answer the question of "How do I end combat without reducing the enemy hp to 0?"
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Alternative Win Conditions/Alternative Win Conditions.md

entries.title: Restraint
entries.content: When creatures don't surrender, but you don’t want to risk killing them, restraints can be used to end the fight. Some situations can naturally be neutralized by dropping a cage in the right place, but such conveniences are rare. More often than not, restraining your foe is going to involves rope and cuffs. Cuffs Grabbing and already-grabbed creature allows you to put cuffs on them. One grab is needed per appendage cuffed. You cannot cuff a creature on your own unless you can grab multiple times per turn, in which case you need another free hand. This provokes AoO. This completely prevents the use of their arms outside of simple item interactions. Cuffs can also be placed on the legs to reduce a creatures speed to 15. Combat maneuvers made against a cuffed creature gain a +10 bonus. Cuffs are difficult to escape, and even harder to break. They typically require outside assistance in order to be removed. Because cuffs are typically made from iron and steel, they can take a significant beating. DC to break (Athletics) or lockpick (Hands) is dependent on the type of material and the lock. A typical forged cuff is DC 40 to break, or 20 to pick. Creatures that are bound can try to free themselves, but take a -5 penalty. Rope Grabbing and already-grabbed creature allows you to tie them with rope. One grab is needed per appendage tied. You cannot tie up a creature on your own unless you can grab multiple times per turn, in which case you need another free hand. This provokes AoO. Rope is easier to destroy than cuffs, but it is also easier to make the restraint more intense. By grabbing one more time, you can prevent the creature from acting completely. Rope tends to be a good way to bind singular creatures, since anybody with a sharp implement can handily free a bound creature. Rope can be destroyed with an attack from any slashing weapon. Using Athletics or Acrobatics, you can pull the ropes apart, but the DC is determined by the athletics bonus of the creature who bound you (30+their athletics bonus). Creatures that are bound can try to free themselves, but take a -5 penalty. This penalty can be ignored with a natural attack suitable for freeing yourself, or a slashing concealed weapon. Container Grabbing and already-grabbed creature allows you to forcefully shove them into an object within reach such as a bag, barrel, or box, so long as they can handily fit in the object. Whether or not they can escape generally depends on the object and the tools the captured creature has on hand. Wooden crates don’t do to well when trying to hold barbarians or crafty characters with crowbars. DC to break (Athletics) is dependent on the type of material. Some objects can be handily dealt with, not requiring a skill check. A knife makes quick work of a bag, and given enough space, hammers break boxes. Creatures that are bound can try to free themselves, but take a -5 penalty.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Alternative Win Conditions/Restraint.md

entries.title: Surrender and Morale
entries.content: During combat there come times when you do not wish to fight, usually when one side is close to victory, but can also happen when creatures are clearly outnumbered or outmatched. How well a surrender feels in a situation usually comes down to a gut feeling, affected by a lot of variables. In order to even consider a surrender to be viable, one or more of the following (or other applicable) conditions should be met… >Numbers advantage >Some other clear advantage, such as terrain, positioning, equipment or a powerful noteworthy ally >Heavily damaged or unconscious allies >Consequences for not surrendering (often death or further prosecution) >Hostages >Whether or not creatures are minions or named characters A surrender requires clear communication, which may not always be possible given the hectic and spread out nature of battle. Some creatures, particularly mindless ones, will not surrender unless specific circumstances are met. Creatures of animal intelligence can be convinced to surrender if you can speak to animals or have wild empathy etc. Surrender can mean forfeiting weapons, fleeing in fear, begging for mercy or something similar. If not all individuals accept the surrender, then the fight continues until they do.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Alternative Win Conditions/Surrender and Morale.md

entries.title: Truce
entries.content: In situations where there are two opposing sides that are generally neutral towards each other or even allies with each other, you can call for a truce. This generally requires important information (we were sent by the merchants guild), certification from an important or recognizable organization (legal documentation) or a special item (an artifact or flag). It could also be appropriate to call for a truce if you are delving into a dungeon and encounter another party, so long as you aren't after the same thing. You might not cooperate, but at least you won't be killing each other. If more information is revealed by talking throughout a battle (wait you're also hunting for the garbooni?), you can also reasonably call for a truce.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Alternative Win Conditions/Truce.md

entries.title: +Combat Maneuver
entries.content: Combat Maneuvers are attacks that target Poise. When making a Combat Maneuver, you must decide which specific form it takes, such as a Topple, Dirty Trick, Shove etc. A successful Combat Maneuver will inflict a condition based on the form you chose. You can combat maneuver an ally automatically. This allows you do things such as to Drag or shove an ally out of danger, but it sets their poise to 1. You can apply bonuses to combat maneuvers to any maneuver you make, even if it doesn't originate from you directly. This means when you successfully shove a creature with Grasping Corpse and you have the Maneuver Master feat, it Provokes from all your allies. The one exception to this rule is effects that make companions. Since they are their own creatures, they don't have your features.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Combat Maneuvers/+Combat Maneuver.md

entries.title: Bully
entries.content: Deal poise damage to a creature that cannot reduce them below 1 poise, with the intention to combo or setup an ally. Bully uses the highest bonus you have to a specific combat maneuver. So if you have a +4 bonus to attack to shove and +8 to grapple, bully is rolled at +8.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Combat Maneuvers/Bully.md

entries.title: Dirty Trick
entries.content: Apply clumsy 1, sickened 1 or blurred vision to a creature. They must spend a standard action to get rid of the condition.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Combat Maneuvers/Dirty Trick.md

entries.title: Disarm
entries.content: Knock a held item out of a creature’s hand, landing at their feet. It takes 30 ft of Movement or a Standard Action to pick up an item off the ground, which Provokes.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Combat Maneuvers/Disarm.md

entries.title: Drag
entries.content: Grab onto a hold of a creature and immediately pull them adjacent to you. You can then move up to 20 ft while holding onto them, and they move into any space you move into during this movement (essentially following behind you during this movement). This is reduced to 10 feet if they are larger than you and you do not possess enough Carry Weight to drag them (typically you can drag double your carry weight). You can choose any space within your natural reach to place the creature in at the end of your drag (both pulling adjacent and positioning a creature around you count against the distance you can drag). Your movement is Elusive against the target of your drag. Special - You must use at least one prehensile limb to initiate a drag on a creature, unless your weapon has the CM (Drag) property.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Combat Maneuvers/Drag.md

entries.title: Grapple
entries.content: Your foe is entangled and Held Down. Your speed is reduced to zero and you pull the creature adjacent to you. The creature cannot Threaten. Each round you can spend your standard action to keep the target in place, dealing the damage of a held weapon automatically. If you don't, the creature is freed. If you are moved out of reach from the target, have your poise reduced to zero, or are otherwise unable to maintain your grip, the target is freed. - Multiple creatures can grapple one creature. The bonus to attack against a target that is entangled from a grapple increases by 2 per additional creature. - You must use at least one prehensile limb to initiate and maintain a grapple on a creature, unless your weapon has the CM Grapple property. - Creatures ignore the attack penalty from partial and total concealment against creatures that they are being grappled by or are grappling.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Combat Maneuvers/Grapple.md

entries.title: Shove
entries.content: Throw your foes into hazards, spells and off cliffs. Push the target 10ft away from you.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Combat Maneuvers/Shove.md

entries.title: Topple
entries.content: Inflict Knocked Down. Deal damage as thought you had Grazed your opponent.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Combat Maneuvers/Topple.md

entries.title: High Ground
entries.content: A Circumstance that you get when you have an elevation edge of at least 10ft over your opponent. You cannot gain high ground by using the Jump action. High ground lets you ignore the Attack penalty from firing ranged attacks in the second range increment.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/Ranged Attack/High Ground.md

entries.title: Long
entries.content: A long range weapon's first range increment is 60ft and second is 120ft.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/Ranged Attack/Long.md

entries.title: Range Increments
entries.content: Most ranged attacks have 2 listed ranges. When you make an attack at a creature within the first listed range (the first range increment), there is no attack penalty. When you make one against a creature in the second listed range (the second range increment), you take a -2 Attack penalty. This penalty can be negated with high ground. Attacks beyond this range are impossible. Whenever you have a feature that increases the the range of your attack, like Artillery, this range increase also applies to the second increment. Some ranged attacks, like spells and supernatural abilities like the Bender elemental blast, have flat ranges, usually 30 or 60 ft. Effects that increase the range of attacks do not affect these features.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/Ranged Attack/Range Increments.md

entries.title: Ranged Attack
entries.content: A Ranged Attack is an Attack Roll made with ranged targeting. This means you can make the attack up to the listed range, not limited by the space you Threaten. These attacks can also be penalized due to Cover. Most physical Ranged Attacks have three listed ranges: Thrown, Spatial Rend and long. Making a Ranged Attack Provokes. Abilities that function from a distance but do not use an Attack Roll are not Ranged Attacks.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/Ranged Attack/Ranged Attack.md

entries.title: Short
entries.content: A medium range weapon's first range increment is 40ft and second is 80ft.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/Ranged Attack/Short.md

entries.title: Thrown
entries.content: A thrown attack is a Ranged Attack that can be made using either STR or DEX. Its first range increment is 20ft and second is 40ft.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/Ranged Attack/Thrown.md

entries.title: +Siege Weapon
entries.content: Siege weapons are attacks that come in the form of enormous weapons of war. This includes ranged attacks like ballista, cannons and enormous rocks, as well as melee attacks like battering rams. Siege weapons are typically placed a tier above most forms of attack, and thus ignore many defensive abilities like parry, Wind Wall or shield actions.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/Siege Weapons/+Siege Weapon.md

entries.title: Ballista
entries.content: Cost: 10,000g Weight: 500 lbs | Defensive Features | | | ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | | HP | 50 | | Saves | Ballista are immune to Will saves and Fortitude saves that cant affect objects. Otherwise, they automatically fail all saves | | Resistance | Pierce | | Offensive Features | | | ------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | Load time | 3 standard actions | | Activation | 1 standard action | | Damage | 4d8 (18) and 20 Poise damage, inflicting Knockdown when poise broken | | Range | 300 ft | | Target/AoE | 1 creatureCreatures within 10 ft of the target take 20 poise damage, inflicting Knockdown when poise broken | | Siege Mode | When making an attack against a creature that has Cover, the cover applies, but it is destroyed after. Same goes for Improved Cover. Conditionally can destroy total cover if the barrier is relatively thin/flimsy. | Since ballista are so time consuming to load, it usually requires a team of 4 people to fire a shot each round. 1 person to load the bolt and aim, 2 to crank the bolt back and 1 to call the shots. The three people loading are treated as taking the Aid action to increase the attack bonus of the person calling the shots, essentially granting the maximum +6 bonus with a full team. Once loaded, the person calling the shots makes an Ranged Attack Roll.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/Siege Weapons/Ballista.md

entries.title: Dwarven Multicannon
entries.content: Cost: 125,000g Weight: 150 lbs | Defensive Features | | | ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | | HP | 50 | | Saves | Multicannons are immune to Will saves and Fortitude saves that cant affect objects. Otherwise, they automatically fail all saves | | Resistance | Pierce, Slash | | Offensive Features | | | ------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | Load time | 1 standard action for every 6 cannonballs | | Activation | 1 FRA | | Damage | 4d8 (18) and 20 Poise damage, inflicting Knockdown when poise broken | | Range | 120 ft | | Target/AoE | Varies (up to 6) | | Siege Mode | When making an attack against a creature that has Cover, the cover applies, but it is destroyed after. Same goes for Improved Cover. Conditionally can destroy total cover if the barrier is relatively thin/flimsy. | Dwarven multicannons are incredibly feats of engineering that allow for rapid fire siege weapon attacks against groups of enemies with some severe limitations to offset the rapid damage output. Only 2 people are needed to upkeep a multicannon, with one maintaining a steady supply of cannonballs (the standard action) and one person calling the shots (the FRA). If infrastructure is not set up to automatically load the cannon, a creature can only load 3 cannonballs a turn. The cannon holds a total of 6. The cannon itself has legs that keep it stable on the ground while its head rotates in a regular pattern in a circle on an axis. This axis is typically left untouched, allowing it to shoot in all directions. It can only shoot once in a direction each round (northeast, north, northwest, southwest, south, southeast). This makes it exceptional when surrounded in targets. The axis can be tilted as a standard action to accommodate for a High Ground or low ground position, but it limits the cannon to firing only 3 times per round in that general direction. As with all siege weapons, all creatures beyond the first are treated as though taking the Aid action to boost attack.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/Siege Weapons/Dwarven Multicannon.md

entries.title: +Types of Attack Rolls
entries.content: Attacks can come in a variety of forms. The most common distinction being melee and ranged attacks. Many techniques have restrictions on the kinds of attacks that can be used to perform them. If you are not rolling, then it is not considered an Attack Roll roll.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/+Types of Attack Rolls.md

entries.title: Melee Attack
entries.content: An attack that is made with against a creature within Reach, that is not a Ranged Attack. Generally speaking, the spaces your Threaten and your reach are the same.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/Melee Attack.md

entries.title: Natural Attack
entries.content: An natural attack is an Attack Roll that is made with a part of a creatures body that naturally dangerous, such as claws or fangs. Exotic creatures have natural attacks that can be all kinds of things, like spitting thorns or tentacle whips. Natural attacks are usually melee attacks, but can sometimes be ranged attacks. Natural attacks always have the Finesse property, letting them use DEX to attack and damage.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/Natural Attack.md

entries.title: Spell Attack
entries.content: An attack that is made as part of casting a spell. Spell attacks can be Melee Attacks or Ranged Attacks. If the attack is melee, it can use STR or DEX to attack. If it is ranged, it must use DEX.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/Spell Attack.md

entries.title: Unarmed Attack
entries.content: An unarmed attack is an Attack Roll that is made with a part of a creatures body that is not traditionally considered a weapon. For example, a headbutt, a punch or a kick. Other body parts can be reasonable used for unarmed attacks in the right circumstance, like a shoulder slam, an elbow or knee. Unarmed strikes are melee attacks. Unarmed attacks are light weapons, allowing them to use DEX to attack and damage. Without special training you do not Threaten with unarmed strikes and they only have 1 base damage.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/Unarmed Attack.md

entries.title: Weapon Attack
entries.content: A weapon attack is an Attack Roll that is made with a manufactured weapon, such as a sword, spear, gun or bow. Weapon attacks can be melee attacks or ranged attacks.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Types of Attack Rolls/Weapon Attack.md

entries.title: Attack Progression
entries.content: Creatures that aren't Dual Wielding have only 1 attack by default. When you use your standard action to make multiple attacks, it's called a Multiattack. You can distribute the attacks to creatures as you see fit, move in between them, before and after them (for certain AoE attacks like cleave, you usually need to stand still). By default a standard attack is considered a simple attack.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Attack Progression.md

entries.title: Attack Roll
entries.content: Attack rolls are any type of harmful effect targeting a creature that requires rolling. This can be spells, swords or trying to push your foe off a cliff. Most attacks assume you use a weapon, a Natural Attack or an Unarmed Attack. Default: d20+ (lvl) +(ability score) +/- (circumstances) In general AC is not a zero sum endeavor. If you successfully meet the AC number, you deal full damage. if you roll 10 higher than the AC number, you Crit. If you roll under the AC number, but not by more than 5, you get a partial hit as you graze your opponent with your attack. Generally speaking, you can only make a single attack with your standard action, but creatures with Multiattack, and special cases like Classes/Fighter/Fighter or Martial Artist, can make multiple on their turn.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Attack Roll.md

entries.title: Crit
entries.content: Critical hits occur when you roll 10 higher than a creature's AC. Crits deal 1.5x damage. Its also worth noting here that all damage multipliers are additive. For example, group type enemies like swarms takes 1.5x damage from AoE. If you cleave and crit, you deal 2x damage.

entries.title: Damage
entries.content: Damage is a value that subtracts from a target's +HP or Poise when the effect takes place. Damage is always assigned a type. The physical damage types are Crush, Slash, and Pierce, and the elemental types are Force, Zap, Fire, Frost, Holy, Necrotic, Poison and Psychic. These are also tags. Poise damage is its own separate thing. If something deals multiple damage types, it usually adds both tags to the effect. For example, a flaming sword will deal Fire and Slash damage with its total damage.

entries.title: Dual Wielding
entries.content: You can make attacks with weapons in both hands simultaneously as long as they both have the light property.
