Whenever you make an attack against a creature (unless +Combat Maneuver) with one of your weapons (the "main hand"), you can make an additional simple attack (that cannot be replaced with any other type of attack, including combat maneuvers) against the same creature with the other weapon (the "off hand") . Because you strike with the 2 weapons in tandem, if the "main hand" attack is enough to defeat a creature, you cannot make an "off hand" attack against a different creature. If you make a special attack that involves making multiple attacks, such as the cleave special attack, you can only make the "off hand" attack against the first creature you attack with it. If the special attack grants some special property to the weapon, like weapon toss allowing you to throw any weapon, it does not do so for the off-hand unless specified otherwise. If you gain additional limbs to wield weapons, you do not get more "main hand" or "off hand" attacks by default. You cannot dual wield with natural attacks or unarmed strikes by default.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Dual Wielding.md

entries.title: Graze
entries.content: Graze is an attack that is not a complete miss. When you roll below the targets AC, but not by more than 5, then the attack does half damage. A graze does not apply any on-hit effects, like bleed. Unless otherwise specified, a graze is considered a miss.

entries.title: Line of Effect
entries.content: A straight line between two places with no Total Cover or impassable obstacles for a given effect grants Line of Effect. This is different from sight, as you may be able to see someone and not Target them (such as through a window).
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Line of Effect.md

entries.title: Simple Attack
entries.content: Simple attacks are only rolling attack and damage, typically with no special effects tied to them. The most common form of simple attack is an Attack of Opportunity. This terminology is important to distinguish since many features will limit attacks by making them simple attacks as opposed to special attacks Special attacks allow for more versatility in play style. Unique weapons, class abilities, techniques and feats can grant these special attacks. Special attacks can usually only be performed once per turn.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Simple Attack.md

entries.title: Special Attack
entries.content: Special attacks allow for more versatility in play style. Weapons, class abilities, techniques and feats can grant these special attacks. Special attacks have a global cooldown of 1 round, meaning they can usually only be performed once per turn. Special attacks universally have the martial tag. A special attack that does not originate from a technique also has the recharge tag, with a cooldown of 1 minute (unless otherwise stated). The most common form is the Base Special Attacks. When used out of combat (for the utility), you can use them an unlimited number of times. With no cooldown. Some techniques will say something to the effect of "does damage" and then goes on. This means the damage of a held weapon.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Attacking/Special Attack.md

entries.title: Target
entries.content: A Targeted ability refers to one where you must select targets to apply the effect, which is not an Attack Roll Roll. Some abilities are particular about what they work on, and will fail if used outside of their scope. You must have Line of Effect and be able to see your target to target them. If you cannot see them, you must target their space with the intention of targeting them.

entries.title: +Death and Resurrection
entries.content: Let's be honest, the purpose of resurrection in TTRPGs is primarily for the benefit of the players. If a GM makes frequent use of the resurrection spells for entities that the players went to great lengths to kill, it heavily undermines their effort and feels terrible, even if plausible. If all it takes is a bunch of money and a cleric, then rich high level players live in a world without death as a consequence for anybody, and that just aint cool in a game about storytelling. Now that we all agree about that, why does resurrection have an expensive material component cost? To punish the players for being unlucky? Why is it locked behind high-level magic? To make playing cleric mandatory? If we only consider the players, then these things become very obvious problems. Players shouldn't be shoehorned into playing specific classes, nor should the money that they worked hard to obtain be required to continue playing the game. The powers that be should instead be very particular about the who gets a second chance. If the players are the main character of the story, then the game's resurrection mechanics should treat them as such. As a replacement for most resurrection effects, use these rules instead. Or don't use resurrection at all. It's your game. Whatever. IMPORTANT NOTE: Please make sure your players are on board with whatever you decide. Nobody likes surprise mechanics. Isn't this just plot armor? The basic premise of this system of resurrection is that you want player death to have immediate consequences: Not being able to complete a mission or delaying the players long enough so that way the villain can complete the ritual. But you don't want them to have long-term consequences: Not being able to play your character. Having to go on lengthy multi-session quests to find someone willing and able to cast the correct spell to bring you back can be fun, but after a few times, it can get old. So in order to achieve the former and avoid the latter, it's recommended that you bake the reason for the players coming back from the dead into the story. Death still matters or course, after all the players will have allies (:< If your players aren't too attached to their characters, then who needs plot armor? Let 'em die. If they are, consider some of the options in this section.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Death and Resurrection/+Death and Resurrection.md

entries.title: Ashen One
entries.content: In dark souls, certain types of creatures, namely the player character, hold a special place in the world's lore. When you die you don't reload the game, you canonically respawn at the location you last rested, leaving behind all your money where you died. You could choose to adopt a similar system where certain types of conditions need to be met for these unique creatures to die for real. Having the characters lose all their money might not be the best approach for a TTRPG: players like their money. But, in souls games you can go back to the place you died and collect the money again, so maybe. This kind of natural respawn mechanic is a good fit for campaigns where you expect your players to encounter dangerous creatures on the regular and your players don't get too discouraged from losing. Meat grinder without the consequences of losing your character for good. I will say though, dark souls is a very fast paced game where you can die every few minutes. In NN, many fights will likely last over an hour which makes the consequence of frequent death a bit more of a hassle.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Death and Resurrection/Ashen One.md

entries.title: Cloning Device
entries.content: You start off your adventures waking up in some alchemist's lab, surrounded in copies of yourself. Whenever you die, there is a fresh body waiting for you back at the lab that your soul gets sucked into. This plays more into having a secret base type thing, and it feels a little less metagame-y than "chosen by god." it also has an interesting mystery component to it. Why were you guys chosen as clones? Whose lab is this? How's that for storytelling with resurrection?
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Death and Resurrection/Cloning Device.md

entries.title: Limited Resurrection
entries.content: Pose a caveat to resurrection magic that imposes a severely limiting factor, such as only being able to revive a creature shortly after it died with some kind of resource attached to it. Maybe even make it a limited resource by tying it to physical items, like scrolls with this effect attached to it. EFFECT | Range | Reach | | -------- | ----------------- | | Target | One dead creature | | Duration | instantaneous | | Save | - | You breathe new life into a recently deceased creature. If the creature has died within the last minute, they are brought back to life at 1 hp and drained 10.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Death and Resurrection/Limited Resurrection.md

entries.title: Tombstone
entries.content: In the event of your characters untimely demise, worry not, for the gods smile upon you. When killed, your body becomes a tombstone with your name and face on it. It weighs as much as you did, including all of your equipment. This tombstone can be carried back somewhere to perform a ritual and bring the character back to life. This can be a place of significance to this god, something generic like a church or something with more story relevance. It can be wherever home base is, or you can require the players to reach certain points in the campaign in order to get their characters resurrected (Ex: Your friend died by the lich's hand, and now, to get them back, you have to destroy his phylactery. Make a temp character!). The basic idea is there: satisfy this entity's conditions and life will be restored.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Death and Resurrection/Tombstone.md

entries.title: Withers
entries.content: In Baldur's Gate 3, there is an NPC that shows up in your camp that has a vested interest in your success. He has powers that aren't like normal characters, chief among them the ability to resurrect fallen allies. You could easily introduce a similar character into your campaign, maybe even the introduction to the campaign is this NPC resurrecting the players. This particular method has the benefit of being less nebulous than divine forces. It gives someone the players can interact with and someone that can give them direction. Whatever the reason they have for resurrecting your players, it's because they need their help.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Death and Resurrection/Withers.md

entries.title: +HP
entries.content: How much hp a player has is largely determined by their class. Creatures have a base hp value of 20, and then a value that they add each LvL, including level one. Classes and creatures all fall under one of the following categories based on their play style. | Class Type | HP/lv | Description | | ---------- | ----- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | Specialist | 6 | Specialist creatures rely almost solely on spellcasting and supernatural abilities. They have a great many skills in their repertoire. They tend to have unusual defensive qualities, but a few solid hits are usually enough to take them down. | | Mixed | 8 | Mixed creatures tend to be somewhere in between martials and specialists. They usually combine Supernatural abilities and spells with weapon strikes or are capable of doing either, but not quite as well as a martial or specialist. | | Martial | 10 | Martials creatures have limited supernatural abilities that aren't directly tied to weapon attacks, however they tend to have a bit more nuance to their strategy than brutes. They usually have high Attack and AC. | | Brute | 12 | Brute creatures rely almost solely on hitting things very hard to solve all their problems. On top of high hp, they usually have other extraordinary defensive qualities like DR, Resistance and Healing. They usually require heavy focus to take down. |

entries.title: Defeat
entries.content: When you take damage that reduces you to zero hp, you are defeated, and must be healed to regain consciousness in a timely manner. You can choose to non-lethally defeat someone, even retroactively after the battle, causing them to survive the encounter (if they don't receive further damage). If this is not the case, they must make a DC 8 luck check or die. Creatures that manage to survive being defeated have to keep making this luck check every time they take damage while defeated. When an enemy healer is present, or other healing options like potions are present, players must be more considerate of how they approach the situation. The GM should consider what the players usually prefer when fighting, mercy or death, or should directly ask players if they want to shoot to kill to avoid situations where the players claim "actually I killed them" once a healer arrives on the scene. When a character recovers from the defeated condition, they are dazed on their next turn. When recovering from the defeated condition, you also gain an Affliction. When a main character (ie usually a player) falls to zero HP, they are not at risk of instant death. Instead of rolling luck checks, a main character gets a stack of Doom when they take damage while defeated. Upon acquiring the 5th stack, they die. Doom stacks are lost on rest. See Recovering from Defeat for what happens when defeated creatures are left to their own devices.

entries.title: Healing
entries.content: Healing is an effect that restores current +HP up to your maximum HP, grants Temp HP, or removes debilitating status conditions. Healing effects do not work on Permanent Status, unless otherwise stated.

entries.title: Immunity
entries.content: A very rare defensive quality. You cannot be affected by a particular Damage type, tag or Status.

entries.title: Natural Healing
entries.content: In the absence of danger, a defeated creature will regain 1 hp after 1 hour. They are staggered until they are healed or they rest. With a proper 8 hours rest, a character restores all their HP, all of their daily use abilities and recover mana burn. You can only rest once each day.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Hitpoints/Natural Healing.md

entries.title: Recovering from Defeat
entries.content: After being Defeated for an hour after the last time it took damage, a creature may attempt to shake off the condition with a STR Roll. On a 10 or higher, they lose the Defeated condition, but as long as their +HP remains 0, they are Staggered. After successfully recovering, the creature is Drained 5.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Hitpoints/Recovering from Defeat.md

entries.title: Resistance
entries.content: Having resistance to a Damage type makes you take half damage from it. Having resistance to a Status effect gives you a +4 bonus to saves against effects with that tag.

entries.title: Rest
entries.content: Eight consecutive hours of sleep. If you sleep in undesirable conditions, or if your rest is interrupted, you do not gain the benefits of rest, and you are exhausted the following day. You can sleep in an extra two hours to negate any interruptions. In order to rest, you must be in some type of sanctuary. A sanctuary is typically something where you and your friends are not the only allies around. It must be comfortable and reasonably quiet. It also does not have enemies lurking just around the corner. Typical sanctuaries include civilization, a camp site nestled in a cave or a region well-defended by allies, such as those protected by a great fairy. When you rest, you restore your HP and Mana Burn. You recover 1 stack of drain. You also can use abilities with limited uses per day, such as recharge abilities.

entries.title: Temp HP
entries.content: Temporary Hitpoints (Temp HP) are gained separately from your +HP. They are lost before your HP, and multiple sources do not stack - only the highest applies. Unless otherwise stated, Temp HP only lasts 1 minute.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Hitpoints/Temp HP.md

entries.title: Weakness
entries.content: Having weakness means you take more damage from a specific Damage type. It is expressed in numerical values like "Holy 3." Denoting that if an attack does holy damage, after all damage calculation, it deals 3 more. Creatures can also have a weakness to something that isn't damage. They have a -4 penalty to Saves against effects of a certain Tag. Just for clarity, weakness functions like DR in that it is applied after everything else. An example... 1. I cast fire blast on ice cube man with weakness 5 fire. 2. Fire blast damage roll is 23 3. Ice cube man passes his save. 4. Half of 23 is 11. 5. 11+5 = 16 final damage

entries.title: Fall Damage
entries.content: Fall damage increases by 3 Crush for every 10 feet you fall, capped at 60 damage. Every 50 feet you are also Drained 5, capped at 20. You take Poise damage equal to the crush damage you took. If this breaks your poise, you are Knocked Down. When determining fall distance, subtract your Jump distance if you take the jump action.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/More Rules/Fall Damage.md

entries.title: Light Level
entries.content: Light levels have 3 distinct categories. Bright Light - A sunny day or a torch lit room. Bright light let's creatures see things clearly. Most creatures can see in bright light without any penalty. Sources of bright light taper off into dim light before turning into darkness. For instance a Torch gives 20 ft of bright light, so it would give an equal amount of distance of dim light. Dim Light - Moonlight or at the edge of your torch light. Many humanoids cannot see well in dim light. Features in dim light appear only as a silhouette with dull, if any, color. Without special senses, creatures have Partial Concealment in dim light. Darkness - The depths of a cave or deep underwater. Few creatures can see clearly in a complete darkness. Some, such as Humans, can't see at all. Those that can (such as with Nightvision or Darkvision) still only see in grayscale. Without special senses, creatures have Total Concealment in darkness.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/More Rules/Light Level.md

entries.title: Overland Travel
entries.content: The default Land Speed of the game is 30, which translates to about 3.4 mph. Assuming the average day is spent sleeping for 10 hours (on shifts with someone keeping watch), walking for 10 hours and eating/taking breaks/setting up camp for the rest, the average person can walk 34 miles per day. This is what I will assume when making the following chart where I do the same process but for creatures with higher/lower speed. Keep in mind, if one member of your party is quite fast, unless they can carry the others, they are slowed down by the rest of the party. Overland travel is determined by the slowest member. This is why many use rocks or teleportation circles to travel in Nack. You can also choose to hustle in order to double your daily distance, but you are exhausted the following day. Doing this for multiple days in a row without rest inflicts drain. You can also choose to rush in order to move distance in 2 hours, after which you are exhausted. This is mostly for getting to important nearby locations when you're on a timer. | Speed | Distance Travelled Per Day (miles) | | ----- | ---------------------------------- | | 20 | 21 | | 25 | 28 | | 30 | 34 | | 35 | 42 | | 40 | 48 | | 45 | 55 | | 50 | 62 | | 55 | 69 | | 60 | 76 | | 65 | 82 | | 70 | 89 | | 75 | 96 | | 80 | 103 | | 85+ | ~7 miles per 5 feet of speed | These speeds can be cut in half if moving through dense jungle, hiking through mountains, trudging through heavy snow or getting bogged down by similar terrain. Note: For reference, in ideal conditions (ie safe and well maintained roads) Rocks can move 600 miles each day, or 1,000 if left on autopilot overnight. Using Exhaustion Exhaustion is a stacking debuff that is intended to be the primary method you inflict general debuffs on the players when travelling through treacherous terrain, hustle and rush during overland travel or when they refuse to rest. Some of these things mention how they inflict exhaustion, but others don't. When they don't... 1. Try and think of a time frame (every time they do x, or every y days). 2. Then have all characters roll Fortitude saves, deciding on a DC based on conditions (usually somewhere around 10-15). Characters that fail are exhausted. 3. Give characters that prepare effectively bonuses, like adv for bringing copious amounts of water into deserts, or +2 for having a thick coat for the cold. Some factors may eliminate the need for a roll entirely. 4. For every time they roll one of these saves, increase the DC by a few points.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/More Rules/Overland Travel.md

entries.title: Rounding Numbers
entries.content: Always round decimals down, minimum 1.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/More Rules/Rounding Numbers.md

entries.title: Size
entries.content: This game is meant to be played on a grid of hexes or squares. Personally, I prefer hexes. Creatures must be represented in such a space. That's why creatures are broken up into size categories. | Size | Space | Reach | Description | Additional Details | | ------- | ---------- | ------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | Tiny | 1 space | 0 ft | Typical for fairies and pets like cats. Tiny creatures are usually around 1 ft tall and weight ANDGain resistance to poise damage from creatures with 3 or less STR. Such creatures cannot poise break massive creatures. | Creatures threaten all spaces they occupy by default. You can enter the space of a creature that is a size category larger or smaller than you. If you enter or leave the spaces occupied by a creature, it Provokes an AoO as though you were leaving a threatened area. If you get attacked this way, the same creature can't also AoO you for leaving their threatened square in the same turn.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/More Rules/Size.md

entries.title: Stacking Damage and Defense
entries.content: When you stack DR and Resistance, add them together in the way that reduces damage the most. When you have multiple sources of increasing damage by a percentage, add the percentages together, do not multiply (ie 1.5x dmg and 1.5x dmg adds together to 2x dmg). This can happen in rare circumstances where you Crit a swarm with cleave for instance. When you have multiple sources of halving damage, such as through a passed Save and Resistance, then you take 50% of the halved damage (20 damage fireblast halved to 10, then halved again to 5). When you have Resistance against an effect that has a multiplier to damage, such as a Crit, apply the resistance to the final damage of the crit (10 damage gunshot, 15 crit, 7 with resistance).
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/More Rules/Stacking Damage and Defense.md

entries.title: +Saving Throw
entries.content: Decide the effectiveness of abilities or effects that do not require an Attack Roll. It is typically the affected creature that rolls the save. In order to consider a saving throw a pass, the affected creature must match or roll higher than the DC of the effect. The DC of a Saving Throw is usually 12 plus the Level of the effect. Saves come in 3 forms: Fortitude saves (based on STR), Reflex saves (based on DEX), and Will saves (based on WIS).
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Saving Throws/+Saving Throw.md

entries.title: Fortitude
entries.content: Fortitude Saves are one of the three primary Saves Rolled to avoid a negative effect. Your Fortitude represents your resistance to physical hardship, such as Venom and concussive attacks. You add STR and LvL to your Fortitude, as well as any bonuses from your +Class.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Saving Throws/Fortitude.md

entries.title: Reflex
entries.content: Reflex Saves are one of the three primary Saves Rolled to avoid a negative effect. Your Reflex represents your reactions and ability to evade or mitigate sudden attacks such as AoE. You add DEX and LvL to your Reflex, as well as any bonuses from your +Class.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Saving Throws/Reflex.md

entries.title: Will
entries.content: Will Saves are one of the three primary Saves Rolled to avoid a negative effect. Your Will represents your mental resilience and ability to resist mental attacks such as Illusions, mind control, and Fear. You add WIS and LvL to your Will, as well as any bonuses from your +Class.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Saving Throws/Will.md

entries.title: Ablaze
entries.content: When a creature is set Ablaze, it takes 2d8 Fire Damage at the start of each turn. Attacking creatures get a +2 bonus to Attack. On every turn after the first, it gets a Reflex save before it takes damage to end the effect. A creature can choose to inflict Knockdown on itself for a +4 bonus to this save. Being submerged in water, getting entombed or taking cold damage also removes this condition. Other things that snuff out flames can reasonably remove ablaze.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Ablaze.md

entries.title: Affliction
entries.content: A type of permanent negative effect that can usually only be removed by taking some time off to rest, recuperate and relax. Afflictions come in tiers from 1-3. Higher tier afflictions usually require more extreme forms of care to remove. They are Permanent Status. See Afflictions for more details.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Affliction.md

entries.title: Anchored
entries.content: Creature cannot use Teleportation effects. Either on themselves or on others. If they attempt to do so, the effect fizzles. It also prevents incorporeal creatures from passing through solid surfaces.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Anchored.md

entries.title: Asleep
entries.content: Creature loses Concentration. This creature is Knocked Down, can take no actions, and cannot be awakened unless it takes damage, is subject to an offensive ability or an ally uses the Aid action to awaken them. The creature is also DD and suffers -10 +Perception. If the creature is asleep through natural means, it can use perception to wake up by noticing something amiss.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Asleep.md

entries.title: Bleeding
entries.content: Bleeding creatures take a specified amount of True Damage at the start of each of their turns for a specified duration. If no duration is specified, it lasts indefinitely. The DC to stop the bleeding (with the +Medicine skill, or magic healing with a CL check) is the attacker's LvL+12. Instead of taking damage, bleed prevents a creature from losing the Defeated condition when healed (unless the healing removes the bleed). Bleed effects do not stack. Only use the most severe effect.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Bleeding.md

entries.title: Blinded
entries.content: Sighted creature takes a -10 penalty to +Perception and treats all creatures as though they had Total Concealment. Creatures' vision range decreases to 10 ft. Creature is also DD.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Blinded.md

entries.title: Blurred Vision
entries.content: Sighted creature takes a -4 penalty to +Perception and treats all creatures as though they had Partial Concealment.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Blurred Vision.md

entries.title: Bone Chilled
entries.content: Healing effects that restore +HP or grant Temp HP are half as effective (minimum 1 healing).
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Bone Chilled.md

entries.title: Charmed
entries.content: Creature cannot attack the entity that inflicted this condition. The entity that inflicted this condition has a +4 bonus on Charisma Skills against this creature. This ends if the creature that inflicted the condition harms the charmed creature. Charmed creatures always interpret what the creature that charmed them is doing in the most positive light: Punches and sword swings are massages and surgeries. Charmed creatures are often willing to do things asked of them provided they don't put the creature in danger or clash with their ethos. After the charm ends, acts are seen for what they truly are. Charmed can be removed by an ally that realizes something is up by taking the Aid action.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Charmed.md

entries.title: Clumsy
entries.content: Creature takes a stacking penalty to Reflex and AC equal to the Clumsy value. This otherwise functions like Shaken. A Clumsy creature cannot ascend using Flight, or things that function similarly to flight, such as a Hover Speed.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Clumsy.md

entries.title: Confused
entries.content: You consider all creatures enemies. Every turn, roll a d10 to determine how you act. Many effects allow you to increase the dice roll with a will save. For confusion effects that last more than 1 round, each subsequent d10 roll gets 2 added to the result. | Roll | Effect | | ---- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | 1-2 | Attack nearest non-Companion creature once with a Simple Attack as a standard action. | | 3-4 | Harm yourself for 2d8 Crush Damage as a Standard Action. | | 5-6 | Consume all movement to wander 15 feet in a random direction. | | 7-8 | Babble incoherently as a Standard Action. | | 9-10 | Confusion ends. | The confusion result is the only thing the creature does on their turn, ie if you roll a 5 you wander and then end turn.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Confused.md

entries.title: Cursed
entries.content: A Cursed creature is suffering from a permanent debilitating condition. Curses cannot be dispelled, but they all have some way to get rid of them. This usually requires some convoluted action most people wouldn't do unless prompted. Can be identified using the "identify curse or affliction" feature in the +Medicine skill.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Cursed.md

entries.title: Dazed
entries.content: Creature loses Concentration. A more severe version of Staggered. Creature cannot take a Standard Action and cannot make AoOs.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Dazed.md

entries.title: DD
entries.content: "Denied Dodge" Creature is unable to respond effectively to attacks due to +Stealth, Feinting, or some other status or Circumstance. Attacks made against a Surprised creature get a +4 bonus. A DD creature also cannot use immediate or instant action in response to actions (like block against a sneak attack). Actions reserved for later with the prepare action can be taken as normal, but occur after an enemy has made themselves known if they are invisible or attacking from stealth. This status effect is also a Circumstance that can be determined on a per-creature or per-attack basis, but can also be a condition that applies to all incoming attacks.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/DD.md

entries.title: Deafened
entries.content: Through loud noises or physical trauma the inner workings of a creatures auditory receptors have been damaged. - You can hear things, but in very muddied and muffled forms, or your hearing is overwhelmed by a loud ringing sound. You cannot understand speech or determine the direction of sounds. This makes you immune to any language effects or similar effects that require your comprehension to function. - You have Disadvantage on +Perception checks and saves made against sonic effects. - Whenever you cast a spell, there you must make a DC 10 luck check or you mess up the words of power and the spell fizzles.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Deafened.md

entries.title: Defeated
entries.content: A defeated creature is Knocked Down and cannot do anything more than a weakly speak while at or below 0 HP. Main characters may crawl at a rate of 10 feet per turn, or move at full speed when another creature uses Aid. When recovering from the defeated condition, a creature immediately loses the knocked down condition and instead becomes Dazed for 1 round. See Defeat for more details.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Defeated.md

entries.title: Divine Shield
entries.content: A shimmering barrier forms around a creature. They are immune to Poise damage and cannot be Grazed while it persists. The next instance of hitpoint damage they suffer is negated in its entirety, also preventing any additional effects that damage would inflict (such as On-hit effects).
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Divine Shield.md

entries.title: Dominated
entries.content: A creature that is dominated is hit with a powerful charm effect, possessed, or controlled by some other means. They are unable to control their own actions and are subject to the will of another creature.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Dominated.md

entries.title: Drained
entries.content: Lowers your current and maximum +HP by 1 for each stack. One point is removed with a day of Rest (or more quickly with +Medicine). A creature with enough drain to decrease their max hp to 0 dies. Some circumstances, such as when a healing effect also inflicts drain for a powerful effect, you can reasonably ignore the current HP reduction.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Drained.md

entries.title: Drowsy
entries.content: At the end of the creature's next turn, it falls Asleep. This can be prevented if it takes any +HP damage.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Drowsy.md

entries.title: Entangled
entries.content: Creature gets -2 AC, and their Land Speed is reduced to zero. This condition usually comes with a means to escape manually with +Athletics. Creatures without the entangled condition receive a +5 bonus on checks to untangle a creature.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Entangled.md

entries.title: Entombed
entries.content: The creature is encased in ice, stone etc. They have Total Cover and are Untargetable. Entombed creatures can only perform mental actions or try to escape (usually requiring an athletics check).
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Entombed.md

entries.title: Exhausted
entries.content: Functions as fatigue, but it can only be removed by through Rest. While exhausted, doing anything that causes you to become fatigued will instead inflict drained 5. The most common way to gain the exhausted condition is to not rest for a night. Exhaustion is considered a permanent status effect. Using Exhaustion Exhaustion is a stacking debuff that is intended to be the primary method you inflict general debuffs on the players when travelling through treacherous terrain, hustle and rush during overland travel or when they refuse to rest. Some of these things mention how they inflict exhaustion, but others don't. When they don't... 1. Try and think of a time frame (every time they do x, or every y days). 2. Then have all characters roll Fortitude saves, deciding on a DC based on conditions (usually somewhere around 10-15). Characters that fail are exhausted. 3. Give characters that prepare effectively bonuses, like adv for bringing copious amounts of water into deserts, or +2 for having a thick coat for the cold. Some factors may eliminate the need for a roll entirely. 4. For every time they roll one of these saves, increase the DC by a few points.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Exhausted.md

entries.title: Fascinated
entries.content: Creature cannot take actions other than watch the source of its fascination. Creature loses this condition after it is attacked, has a +Saving Throw inflicted on it, or succeeds on a +Perception check to notice a creature in its pockets or otherwise taking an offensive action. If the source of its fascination is no longer visible (or able to be sensed for other types of fascination), the condition also ends. After this happens, the creature temporarily becomes immune to fascination for 1 minute.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Fascinated.md

entries.title: Fatigued
entries.content: - Cannot Core Rules, How to Play/Actions/Common Actions/Full Round Actions/Charge or Dash. - Jump distance is halved (minimum 5). This is applies after all other jump modifiers (like Enhanced Jump) - -4 +Athletics, +Perception and +Acrobatics. Removed after 10 minutes of inactivity. Doing anything that causes you to become fatigued again while already fatigued instead causes you to become Exhausted. Note - Because fatigue is relatively easy to remove, it is intended to be a temporary status effect. If you want to start racking up debuffs for arduous journeys, see Exhausted.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Fatigued.md

entries.title: Frightened
entries.content: Creature cannot move closer to the source of its fear. It has disadvantage on attacks and opposed skill checks against the source of its fear.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Frightened.md

entries.title: Frosty
entries.content: Creature is covered in ice. Creature has Resistance to Fire Damage and -15 Land Speed. Attacks against a frosty creature get +2 Attack. This status ends when they take physical or fire damage.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Frosty.md

entries.title: Glowing
entries.content: Creature emits Bright Light with a 5 ft radius, causing it to suffer -10 +Stealth. Creatures attacking a glowing creature get a +2 bonus to Attack. The attack boost does not stack with Ablaze.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Glowing.md

entries.title: Grounded
entries.content: Through bent wings, malfunctioning magic, or disorientation, the creature is unable to stay airborne. Creatures currently flying immediately plummet and provoke AoOs, but retain enough flight to avoid Fall Damage. This also affects Hover speeds.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Grounded.md

entries.title: Held Down
entries.content: If the creature is knocked down, then it cannot remove the condition (ie stand up) until the held down condition is removed. A creature who is grappled is automatically also Held Down
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Held Down.md

entries.title: Invincible
entries.content: Creature's +HP cannot be reduced.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Invincible.md

entries.title: Invisible
entries.content: You cannot be detected by sight. This grants you Total Concealment against any creature that primarily uses sight. You must still use +Stealth to hide your presence and specific location, or else creatures will still be able to attack your space to try and hit you.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Invisible.md

entries.title: Knocked Down
entries.content: Creature takes a -30 penalty to speed and attacks get a +2 bonus to attack against the creature for 1 round, or longer if Held Down.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Knocked Down.md

entries.title: Mana Burn
entries.content: An ability that reduces maximum Mana. Many features throughout the game allow for more powerful effects, but do so at the cost of mana burn. Unless otherwise specified, mana burn decreases only your maximum mana and is not an additional cost to an ability. For example, something like cure wounds costs 1 mana. You decided to mana burn to overheal, it still only costs 1 mana, but your max mana is also reduced by 1. The default value for mana burn is 1. Mana burn can stack with itself, eventually reducing your max mana to 0. Having 0 max mana inflicts the mana starvation Affliction. An ability with no mana cost that has mana burn decreases your current mana by an equal value as well. Bestiary creatures that don't use mana instead drain equal double to the amount they mana burn.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Mana Burn.md

entries.title: Mindless
entries.content: Creature does not have any capacity for reasoning. It performs basic motions regardless of external stimuli. Mindless creatures do not possess an INT score. This status grants Immunity to Mind-Affecting effects.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Mindless.md

entries.title: Pacified
entries.content: The creature cannot take aggressive actions against enemies with intent to inflict damage or Status or assist its allies in inflicting damage.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Pacified.md

entries.title: Possessed
entries.content: A creature who is possessed has another soul inside their body that can exert control over the body. The amount of control can differ depending on the effect from just using their eyes, to taking control of the entire body. In any case, the possessing creature usually leaves their body behind Stunned and vulnerable to attack, unless stated otherwise. The intruder can willingly stop possessing the creature and return to their own body as a free action.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Possessed.md

entries.title: Shaken
entries.content: Creature takes a penalty to Will and DC equal to the Shaken value. Adding new stacks of Shaken gives all stacks the longest duration of all the effects. Usually, Shaken wears off after 1 minute out of combat.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Shaken.md

entries.title: Sickened
entries.content: Creature takes a penalty to Fortitude and Attack equal to the Sickened value. Otherwise functions like Shaken. Sickened usually wears off after 1 minute out of combat.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Sickened.md

entries.title: Silenced
entries.content: Creature cannot speak, including to cast spells or communicate information via +Monster Lore. Spells that state an absence of speech can still be cast.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Silenced.md

entries.title: Siphoned
entries.content: The creature is severed from its magic. It is not capable of casting Spells, Concentration, or using its activating Su abilities. Constant effects, effects from items, or effects that were already cast remain in effect.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Siphoned.md

entries.title: Staggered
entries.content: - Creature loses its swift action. - -15 speed. - Creature can only use one attack in its Multiattack.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Staggered.md

entries.title: Starvation
entries.content: Creature has not eaten enough for multiple days in a row. Starving creatures are exhausted and become drained 5 every day then do not eat. If you become drained 25 as a result of starvation, you die.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Starvation.md

entries.title: Stasis
entries.content: Creature is frozen in time, or some other particularly powerful effect. They are Untargetable, Unassailable, Unstoppable and Invincible. They cannot take any actions unless otherwise stated.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Stasis.md

entries.title: Status
entries.content: A status effect is a condition that is placed on a creature to apply CC, change their rolls, and otherwise modify how they interact with the world... For better or for worse.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Status.md

entries.title: Stunned
entries.content: Creature loses Concentration. A more extreme form of being Dazed. Creature cannot take any actions or movement. Creature is DD.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Stunned.md

entries.title: Submerged
entries.content: Target is completely surrounded in liquid. This triggers Aquatic Combat and Improved Cover.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Submerged.md

entries.title: Summoning Sickness
entries.content: Creature is drained 10 and cannot be called from Summoning Contract again until they are no longer drained.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Summoning Sickness.md

entries.title: Taunted
entries.content: A Taunted creature must use all their attacks on the target of the taunt, and must include the target in all of their special attacks (Spells, Su, etc.). A taunted creature can only move if the movement puts them closer to the creature that taunted it. A creature taunted by 2 or more creatures may decide which they'd like to attack (assuming they attack at all). More extreme forms of taunt may restrict the types of actions the creature can take while taunted.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Taunted.md

entries.title: Time Out
entries.content: A creature in Time Out has lost the will to fight, and has surrendered to you. They cannot inflict harmful effects on anybody or anything. They also cannot cast spells without your permission. They meekly follow your commands (ex. follow me), but won’t do anything against their ethos (kick that puppy) or that is dangerous (jump off that cliff) without extreme circumstances. If you or your allies attack the creature or cast a harmful spell on it, the condition ends. Treat it like a slightly more convenient form of unconsciousness if you want to interact with the creature after the combat. Many minion type creatures will acquire this status effect when their leader is defeated.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Time Out.md

entries.title: Unassailable
entries.content: Creature ignores penalties that result from Circumstance or Status, like the +Perception penalty from being blind.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Unassailable.md

entries.title: Unstoppable
entries.content: Creature cannot be moved by force, and can move and take actions normally regardless of any Status like stun or entangle.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Unstoppable.md

entries.title: Untargetable
entries.content: Creature cannot be targeted, such as if it has Total Concealment.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Untargetable.md

entries.title: Wet
entries.content: Gain weakness to Electric and Cold Damage. When a wet creature takes Electric or Cold damage, the condition ends. Being wet is something that can come up often in the environment. Only by being drenched in heavy rain, submerged in water or something similar can one be considered wet to the point where this has a mechanical effect.
entries.path: Core Rules, How to Play/Status Effects/Wet.md

entries.title: Air
entries.content: Effect manipulates air to produce its effect. This is a non-Damage elemental tag.

entries.title: Buff
entries.content: An effect with the buff tag grants a boon to a creature, or a shared effect. If an unwilling creature has a buff or used on it, it can choose to deny it. You can end a buff effect on yourself as an instant action, unless the buff says otherwise. The effects of a buff are suppressed when a creature is defeated, with the effect resuming once the condition is removed.

entries.title: Charm
entries.content: Charm effects allow the creature inflicting it to exert some degree of control over others. All charm effects are also Mind-Affecting. The charmed condition is a common one, but some charm effects are more or less extreme. Charm effects cause the creature to interpret what the creature that charmed them is doing in the most positive light: Punches and sword swings are massages and surgeries. Charmed creatures are often willing to do things asked of them provided they don't put the creature in danger or clash with their ethos. After the charm ends, acts are seen for what they truly are.

entries.title: Companion
entries.content: In media it's very common for creatures to have minions, squires and sidekicks. This is what the companion system is for. Like most other content, the companion system is ill-suited for large quantities of creatures, like armies. It's meant to be more compact. Something to take with you on the go for your adventure. This could also reasonably extend to temporary allies that join your party. Companions are frequently tied to your class, abilities and items. Any such ability is marked with this tag. If an ability has the companion tag, it summons an ally, or otherwise serves some kind of interpersonal function. Companions and Defeat What happens to your companions when you are defeated? - All your companions lose your CHA to attack - Your companions lose any teamwork feats you were giving them. - If your companion was created by some type of magical effect, such as a Summoning Contract or a Mud Buddy, then it disappears immediately when you are defeated. - If your companion exists outside of you and your magic (such as a squire or Beast Companion) then it does not disappear. - At GM discretion, lesser companions (like minions) may even flee when their leader is defeated More rules for companions - You can only have 2 companions, or 1 if you have -1 CHA. - Companions always act on your turn if you are present. There is no separation between your turns, you all act in the same Initiative. - Companions gain bonuses or penalties based off of your CHA. - If you are not currently sharing your teamwork feat gained through CHA with anybody, or you hate them and like your Snow Man better, you can grant it to a companion that you summon as a free action as part of creating them. - If a feature generates a specific type of companion, like a Beast Companion or Mud Buddy, then you cannot have another companion of the same type unless otherwise specified. - If a companion is generated temporarily (such as through a technique or a Su ability), then it has a "leash" range of 120 ft. In addition, you need line of sight to your companion. The first turn it exists beyond this range it slowly starts to fade away. At the end of the second it disappears. Acquiring companions outside of class As stated previously, companions are most frequently obtained by summoning them via spells or as core class features. That does not mean this is necessary however. If for story reasons you encounter characters willing to follow you, and they are minions relative to you, you can take them under your wing and give them companion status. If they later gain levels such that they are no longer a minion, they no longer qualify for being a companion.

entries.title: Concentration
entries.content: An effect with the concentration tag requires some degree of upkeep from the caster and typically has a duration. Concentration effects end if the caster is defeated, dazed, put Asleep, or otherwise completely prevented from taking any actions. You can also cease concentration as an instant action. If you cast a spell with concentration, you must hold the staff in order to maintain your concentration. Being disarmed of a staff you are using to concentrate ends the effect. You can normally only have one concentration effect at a time.

entries.title: Contingent
entries.content: The effect can be cast ahead of time with a specific trigger for when it activates. A creature can only have a single contingent effect active on them at once. The effect manifests itself in a very obvious way. A creature can identify what the contingency is without seeing it in action with an appropriate skill check, usually Identify Active Spell, but with a +5 increase to the DC.

entries.title: Crush
entries.content: Crush is a Damage type Tag, which represents bludgeoning attacks, shockwaves, and blunt force trauma.

entries.title: Curse
entries.content: Curse effects inflict permanent debilitating conditions on creatures. Curses cannot be dispelled, but they all have some way to get rid of them. This usually requires some convoluted action most people wouldn't do unless prompted. Can be identified using the Identify Affliction or Curse (P) feature in the +Medicine skill. Many curses include visible marks on the creature, even if not explicitly stated in the ability description.

entries.title: Dark
entries.content: Effect either creates Darkness, or has special interactions when in it.

entries.title: Dismissible
entries.content: Dismissible (D) effects can be ended prematurely by the user as a Standard Action. Note that voluntary effects (such as buffs) can be dismissed Instantly without this tag.

entries.title: Earth
entries.content: Spell manipulates earth to produce its effect. This is a non-Damage elemental tag.

entries.title: Elusive
entries.content: This action doesn't provoke an AoO. This most frequently applies to Movement, but can apply to anything that usually Provokes, such as making a Ranged Attack.

entries.title: Emotion
entries.content: An Emotion effect is a Mind-Affecting effect that relies on manipulating the target's emotions.

entries.title: Fear
entries.content: Usually inflicts Shaken or Frightened. All fear effects are also Mind-Affecting.

entries.title: Figment
entries.content: Figments create creatures, objects, and terrain that don’t truly exist. As long as the creature does not directly interact with a figment, it believes it to be real. If a creature figures out that it's fake and tells its allies, or demonstrates by moving through it, they no longer believe it to be real. Directly interacting requires doing things like walking into a wall or attacking a creature, upon which the creature rolls a Will save. A failure indicates the creature will try to run around an obstacle or that they missed a creature. If used to satisfy the conditions of a surrender (such as making a spooky monster), the creatures affected get a will save to negate it. They have +6 on this save if they saw you cast the spell.

entries.title: Fire
entries.content: Fire is a Damage type Tag, representing destruction through heat melting metal or cooking flesh.

entries.title: Force
entries.content: Magic is a Damage type Tag, which represents damage that is supernatural in origin, forces rending and unmaking through means that cannot be described by physical processes.

entries.title: Frost
entries.content: Frost is a Damage type Tag, representing the disruption of movement and natural processes through reduction in temperature and application of cold beams, wind and snow.

entries.title: Glamer
entries.content: A Glamer changes a subject’s sensory qualities, making it look, feel, taste, smell, or sound like something else, or nothing at all.

entries.title: Holy
entries.content: Holy is a Damage type Tag, representing attacks against the soul or convictions, or divinely-sourced retribution. Usually particularly effective against Undead and Fiends.

entries.title: Language-Dependent
entries.content: Language-Dependent effects require the target to understand your spoken words, or requires some form of clear communication that mimics language (like Telepathy).

entries.title: Lewd
entries.content: This effect riles up a creature and makes them horny in some way shape or form. A creature affected by something with a lewd tag cannot be the target of another such effect for 1 minute after the previous effect wears off. All lewd effects are also Mind-Affecting Common in Brazzeria (T) spells.

entries.title: Light
entries.content: Effect either creates light, or has special interactions when in it. If unspecified, it creates Bright Light in its area of effect for one round, or on the spaces where something was targeted.

entries.title: Martial
entries.content: Martial effects allow the user to deliver the effect using the Attack and Damage and On-hit effects of a wielded weapon, even if it mentions using another form of attack. Martial techniques and Special Attacks by default ALWAYS have the martial tag. It is not listed to save time/space.

entries.title: Mind-Affecting
entries.content: Mind-Affecting effects are a more general group that include things like Fear effects, Charms, and more. Creatures that are Mindless cannot be affected by mind-affecting effects.

entries.title: Necrotic
entries.content: Necrotic is a Damage type Tag, representing the decay of flesh, poisons, and the organic disruption of life processes. May bolster Undead.

entries.title: Outplay
entries.content: This effect bypasses AC, poise and saving throws to (almost) automatically deal damage.

entries.title: Overheal
entries.content: This Healing effect can heal past your maximum hp. Any excess healing is converted into Temp HP

entries.title: Pattern
entries.content: Like a Figment, a Pattern creates an image that others can see, but a pattern also affects the minds of those who see it or are caught in it. Common effects include Fascination or Blurred Vision. Pattern effects are also Mind-Affecting.

entries.title: Persistent
entries.content: Persistent Effects have an initial effect and an effect that persists over time. This type of effect cannot deal its persistent effect on the same round as its initial effect (usually). Persistent effects end preemptively if the user is Defeated. There are some effects, like the Skyman's Elemental breath that have an initial effect that is different from the persistent effect. Such effects usually happen if the enemy stays within the area at the end of their turn. This is a special exception to persistent tag not being able to deal damage multiple times in the same round. Dev Note: Persistent effects that do the same things as their initial effect (like Wall of Fire) are not something you want happening multiple times a turn. Don't let players weasel their way into dealing double damage.

entries.title: Pierce
entries.content: Pierce is a Damage type Tag, and represents force applied across a small surface area, usually by pointed objects.

entries.title: Poison
entries.content: Poison is a Damage type Tag, representing an toxic or caustic substance that melts away the body as well as objects.

entries.title: Polymorph
entries.content: Polymorph effects transform creatures into other creatures. Many of the features granted from polymorph effects are based off the Shapeshifter's class features. You retain the ability to speak while polymorphed. When transforming using a polymorph effect, it can fall under one of the following to determine the nature of your transformation (some can be multiple). Chimeric Polymorph - Your form stays roughly the same, but acquires some features of the new creature that you're transforming into, such as claws, fur, a long tongue etc. When you chimeric polymorph, you do not lose access to any of your gear. True Polymorph - Your form completely changes to the form mentioned in the ability. You completely lose access to all of your equipment. There are only a handful of features recognizable from your previous self, such as eye color. Baleful Polymorph - This form of polymorph is inflicted upon you rather than willingly used. It functions very similarly to true polymorph, but provides fewer tangible benefits and often includes debilitating Status. You lose access to your equipment, cannot speak, cast spells or activate Su abilities. Note: "Losing access to your gear" means you can no longer activate any abilities it has (such as a Special Attack), or wield any weapons/spell focus items. If a piece of gear gives a passive bonus (such as armor's AC or a shield's poise) then you still maintain that while true/baleful polymorphed.

entries.title: Prepared
entries.content: This technique does not require any form of activation to use. It provides its benefit constantly. If a tag is Sp or Su, it can be dispelled. In such a case, the ability must be reactivated as a swift action.

entries.title: Psychic
entries.content: Psychic is a Damage type Tag, representing an assault one a creature's mind. Usually ineffective against Mindless creatures.

entries.title: Recharge
entries.content: Recharge abilities have a specific criteria or time required until they can be used again. If no such criteria or duration is listed, then it is assumed that the recharge is resting, making it a 1/day ability.

entries.title: Scrying
entries.content: Scrying effects create a specific type of magic sensor, often from remote locations, usually for the purpose of spying on people. The sensor allows you to see and hear anything with any of your senses, so long as it is in the specified range the effect mentions. While scrying, your body is Stunned. You can return back to your body as a standard action. You are aware of any physical sensation your body experiences while scrying. Scrying sensors bit tied directly to creatures are clearly visible to the naked eye. They usually take the form of an eye. Sensors that can move can use the caster's +Stealth bonus to hide as they move. Scrying is a Concentration effect.

entries.title: Sigil
entries.content: The effect puts magical text on a surface with some kind of delayed effect, usually triggering when a creature walks within a certain radius of the sigil. A sigil must be clearly visible in order to function. If it is obscured, then it is deactivated until it is unobscured. Most sigils have the ability to apply a passphrase that, when spoken aloud, allows a user to move through the area without triggering the sigil. Once triggered, a sigil usually disappears. Any status effects inflicted remain for the usual duration.

entries.title: Slash
entries.content: Slash is a Damage type Tag, and represents damage dealt by cuts, chops, and slices with a sharp or thin weapon.

entries.title: Sonic
entries.content: A damage type centered around being incredibly loud.

entries.title: Subtle
entries.content: The effect has a chance for nobody to notice it. If the target creature fails its save, then the effect goes unnoticed. The DC to Identify Spell Being Cast with the subtle tag increases by 5. If someone is watching you and suspects you are using a subtle spell in their presence, they can use Detect Hidden Presence to get a +Perception check at -10 against the Identify Spell DC of the effect. They must still identify the spell if they want to know its effects, but usually knowing someone is trying to cast spells on you without you knowing is evidence enough of foul play.

entries.title: Tag
entries.content: Tags are descriptors that let you apply general rules to similar effects. Tags are not just limited to spells, but spells do commonly use tags. Certain tags may only effect specific parts of an ability. Use discretion when needed. See: School tags Abnegation Conjuration Divination Evocation Illusion Transmutation Regular tags Air Bond Buff Caloric Charm Companion Concentration Contingent Curse Dismissible Damage Dark Earth Elusive Fear Figment Glamer Language-Dependent Light Martial Mind-Affecting Multi-Strike Pattern Persistent Core Rules, How to Play/Tags/Polymorph Scrying Sigil Subtle Teleportation Core Rules, How to Play/Tags/Water

entries.title: Teleportation
entries.content: Teleportation effects allow the user, and sometimes their allies, to instantly travel between two locations. Teleportation tends to be very limited in scope, bringing you to very specific predetermined places or being wildly off-course if not. You must have line of sight to the location you are teleporting to.

entries.title: Upcast
entries.content: Upcast functions differently depending on whether it is attached to a martial or magic technique. For martial techniques, you spend additional mana and treat it as a higher level technique for a greater effect. Spells have universal thresholds where, if your Caster Level (CL) is high enough, you get bonus effects. These usually happen every 2, or every 4 caster levels, with some spells, they have a unique threshold that unlocks entirely new effects. Example... Ligma Beam Level 1 Nack Tags: Upcast Action: Standard | Range | 60 ft | | -------------- | ---------- | | Target/AoE | 1 creature | | Duration | - | | Save | - | Make a Ranged Spell Attack against a creature. On-hit they contract ligma. Upcast - Every 4 CLs, fire an additional beam at a different creature.

entries.title: Water
entries.content: Effect manipulates water to produce its effect. This is a non-Damage elemental tag.

entries.title: Zap
entries.content: Zap is a Damage type Tag, which represents ionization and burning through the use of electricity.

entries.title: +Other Utility
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Other Utility/+Other Utility.md

entries.title: Anchoring Paint
entries.content: 800g (DC 20) For creatures who dislike pesky mages, plants that drain mana can be mixed with paint in order to create a liquid that can be spread over surfaces. Typically the paint comes with instructions on how you draw the magic circles required to activate its potential. A single can is enough to cover a 20 ft radius in a magic circles. Creatures cannot teleport to any surface that is warded by anchoring paint, or the air above it. After the paint has stopped an instance of teleportation, it rapidly begins to evaporate over the course of an hour.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Other Utility/Anchoring Paint.md

entries.title: Barbarian Chew
entries.content: 600g (DC 16) These plants are rare occurrences in mountainous regions, but stand out due to their red leaves year round. With a little bit of alchemical alteration, they inspire fury in those that chew (and often consume) them. For the rest of the day, you cannot use \[concentration] effects. The next time you rage today you gain 10 additional speed. When this enhanced rage ends, you cannot rage again until you rest.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Other Utility/Barbarian Chew.md

entries.title: Embalming Fluid
entries.content: 150g (DC 15) A slightly less extreme version of flash freeze, adapted for a specific purpose. Often used by morticians to make funeral rites more pleasant. Application of this ointment on a corpse prevents its decomposition and negates its stench for 1 week. With 2 doses the embalming lasts a month. With 3 doses, and a creature trained in the medicine skill, it can last many years.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Other Utility/Embalming Fluid.md

entries.title: Flare
entries.content: 70g (DC 12) By mixing fuel with an oxidizer, all you need to make light is a spark. A flare provides a source of light that doesn't rely on fire, making it an ideal short term light source, or wet adventures. With a quick twist (swift action) to activate the chemical reaction, flares gives off light like a torch (20 ft radius bright) for 30 minutes. Flares are more resilient than torches in that they can be tossed around, even in water, without worry of the light going out. With such a simple triggering mechanism, flares can easily be attached to trip wires, or have some kind of specific firing mechanism similar to a firearm to activate it while launching it high in the sky, or both (just as examples).
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Other Utility/Flare.md

entries.title: Flash Freeze
entries.content: 50g (DC 11) Creating flash freeze requires the scale of a frost dragon to create, but the scale is a reusable component. Only small quantities of this substance can be created and contained at once. When poured over water, the oil pools on the surface and takes 1 round to spread out from the point of origin in a 20-foot radius. At the start of your next turn, the oil flash-freezes the surface of the water, creating an ice sheet over the affected area. Any 5-foot square of this ice can support ~500 pounds of weight. Weight in excess of this amount causes the entire sheet to crack and quickly break up. This ice sheet becomes unstable and breaks up on its own after 1 hour, or faster in a hot climate. This item has no significant effect when used against creatures.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Other Utility/Flash Freeze.md

entries.title: Perfume
entries.content: 90g (DC 13) Perfumes are powerful mixtures of chemicals with distinct and long-lasting scents, made out of a wide variety of substances. Whether or not they are appealing is up to taste however. You can apply a dose of perfume to yourself as a standard action, and it lasts for a few hours, provided you are not drenched in other liquids or other powerful smelling substances. The first time you roll a persuasion check or deception check to lie against someone, roll serendipity. If you roll 17 or higher, the creature likes the smell, giving you a reroll against them for such checks. Creature's with the scent special quality tend to find most perfumes garish and unpleasant, increasing the DC to 20. Creatures without a sense of spell are unaffected by perfume. You can only benefit from a specific type of perfume's benefit once per creature, requiring you to spice things up during your next interaction if you want the reroll again. By succeeding on a monster lore check against a creature, or just asking people who may know such things, you can figure out a type of perfume they enjoy, bypassing the serendipity check entirely. Can also be flavor texted as a breath spray or something similar.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Other Utility/Perfume.md

entries.title: Salve of Slipperiness
entries.content: 500g (DC 15) Oils and secretions of all kinds can be used to make these salves. They are the most effective lubricant on the market, preventing friction in all kinds of scenarios.  Applying it to a creature takes 3 rounds and a thorough rub down. It temporarily gives them DR 10 vs grapples and the compression quality for 10 minutes.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Other Utility/Salve of Slipperiness.md

entries.title: Smelling Salt
entries.content: 20g (DC 10) A form of mineral too delicate for ore processing that requires an alchemist's touch to unlock its true potential. These crystals snap people awake in a hazy stupor when inhaled. Replaces the defeated condition with the time out condition until they restore hp, they take damage or 10 minutes pass.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Other Utility/Smelling Salt.md

entries.title: Super Goo + Super Solvent
entries.content: 1,000g (DC 25) Note: This section mentions 2 different but interrelated items. Must be purchased separately. A gooey substance gathered from goo dragons condensed and then separated into 2 components. Super goo takes all of the sticky qualities of a goo dragon and amplifies them. When exposed to open air for 1 round without being disturbed, it can bind any 2 surfaces to each other permanently. Nothing is stopping the objects from being torn or destroyed, however. Super solvent can negate the adhesive properties of super goo, as well as other "sticky" things like spider webs.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Other Utility/Super Goo + Super Solvent.md

entries.title: +Potions
entries.content: Potions are small flasks that can be downed in one gulp and usually provide some type of positive benefit to the consumer.

entries.title: Agility Potion
entries.content: 800g (DC 20) Rabbit's feet are a key component in creating agility potions. Other creatures known for swiftness are sometimes used as well. Gives a +10 bonus to speed and a +4 bonus to acrobatics checks for 1 minute, after which, the creature is fatigued. This effect is a \[buff].
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Potions/Agility Potion.md

entries.title: Allnight
entries.content: 120g (DC 14) Allnight is made from crushed nocturnal insects. The stimulant is similar to caffeine, but cranked up to 11. it is very easy to tell when someone is under the effects of all night due to their constant twitching/jittering and inability to remain focused. Lasts 8 hours. You suppress the negative effects of fatigue and cannot fall asleep or be put to sleep, but take a -2 penalty to all skill checks and cannot do anything with the \[concentration] tag. You also do not gain the benefits of rest while under the effects of allnight.

entries.title: Antitoxin
entries.content: 20g (DC 10) Anti toxin is a liquid that can be drank or directly applied to wounds as a standard action, depending on the type of poison. When administered it halts the progression of Venom for 1 hour. Using multiple antitoxins on the same creature in the same 24 hour period has no effect.

entries.title: Bloodblock
entries.content: 150g (DC 15) Bloodblock requires extracting toxins from creatures like mosquitoes and removing the itchiness. Many alchemists do this by crushing large quantities of common mosquitoes and using chemical separation processes. Can be used to substitute for a med kit when treating bleeding creatures. Gives a +4 bonus said checks.

entries.title: Gillweed
entries.content: 90g (DC 13) Gillweed is a plant commonly found in swamps at the waters edge. This potion must be inhaled instead of drank. Doing so is very uncomfortable and staggers the creature for 1 round. The potion allows a creature to breathe underwater as well as air for 8 hours.

entries.title: Healing Potion
entries.content: 700g (DC 18) The jizzle when it is not acclimated to a particular creature consumes resources from the creatures body to immediately speed up recovery. Immediately restores 5 hp. By increasing the DC by 4, you can double the price and increase its healing by 5. This doubling in price/+4 DC is cumulative (DC 22 for 700g, DC 26 1400g, DC 30 for 2800g etc) T3 and above potions are considered rare items due to their high craft DC.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Potions/Healing Potion.md

entries.title: Mana Potion
entries.content: 700g (DC 18) By melting down mana crystals, they can be converted into a tasty sweet potion that can be addictive. Immediately restores 1 mana, but you mana burn 1, reducing your maximum mana. Normally mana burn also reduces current mana, but to keep the math simple, increase current mana by 1 and reduce max by 1. By increasing the DC by 4, you can double the price and increase its restorative effects and mana burn by 1. This doubling in price/+4 DC is cumulative (DC 22 for 700g, DC 26 1400g, DC 30 for 2800g etc) T3 and above potions are considered rare items due to their high craft DC.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Potions/Mana Potion.md

entries.title: Tongues Potion
entries.content: 800g (DC 20) Tongues potions are made using the saliva of particularly chatty or loud creatures. When consumed, the potion allows you to sound like any creature. However, this drought is missing one final ingredient: The tongue of the creature you wish to sound like. Without this key ingredient, the potion is useless. Most tongues potions are sold without the final key ingredient, but alchemy stores tend to have a few preserved tongues lying around. Potions that already sound like a specific creature are incredibly rare and exorbitantly, but more general things like "orc" or "imp" might be available for sale. Depending on the rarity of the tongue in question, price could increase by 10% for something common like orc, or increase by 1,000% with the tongue of a prince. Roundabout numbers, decide as appropriate for situation. Big rare=big price.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Potions/Tongues Potion.md

entries.title: Trollblood Potion
entries.content: 800g, (DC 20) By scraping off the natural excretions of a troll's skin, trollblood potions can be made. When consumed, the creature gains regeneration 1 for 1 minute. This effect is suppressed for 1 turn if the creature takes fire/poison damage.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Potions/Trollblood Potion.md

entries.title: +Thrown Concoctions
entries.content: Thrown concoctions are alchemical items that can be used as thrown weapons in combat. Some thrown concoctions explode on impact, requiring a saving throw instead of an attack.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Thrown Concoctions/+Thrown Concoctions.md

entries.title: Alchemist Fire
entries.content: 500g (DC 14) Alchemist fire is one of the first things taught to alchemists and only requires a handful of common chemicals. When exposed to open air, this combination of chemicals ignites. Creatures within 5 ft are set ablaze (DC 14 reflex negates).
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Thrown Concoctions/Alchemist Fire.md

entries.title: Bottled Lightning
entries.content: 500g (DC 14) Typically alchemists create bottled lightning on stormy days, with some type of lightning rod to attract the lightning. They treat the interior of a bottle with a thin layer of a rubbery insulator and extract the stored electrical power into it. When destroyed, the lightning lashes out at nearby creatures. Creatures within 5 ft take 2d6 (7) zap damage (DC 14 reflex negates).
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Thrown Concoctions/Bottled Lightning.md

entries.title: Chloroform
entries.content: 800g (DC 20) Chloroform is mostly a product of a handful of mushrooms and seaweeds, its properties make it useful in a variety of alchemical creations, but the most well known use is its ability to knock creatures out. A handful of spells and plant creatures have adopted use of this "sleep powder" to incapacitate creatures temporarily. Alchemists have figured out how to manufacture chemicals that mimic this, but are significantly less powerful. Carefully sealed in glass jars, chloroform is used as a projectile weapon. Make a thrown attack against a creature or a melee attack to smash it against them. The creature must make a DC 20 fortitude save or become drowsy and a bit numb. This gives them blurred vision and 5 temp hp. If you critically strike the creature, it lands in their face and the has disadvantage on its save. By smashing a second glass jar against an already drowsy creature, they are forced to make an additional save. If they fail, they are put to sleep for 1 round.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Thrown Concoctions/Chloroform.md

entries.title: Flash Powder
entries.content: 400g (DC 11) When thrown with enough force, a bag of flash powder reacts violently with a flash of light. Thrown at a creature's feet, it blinds for 1 round (DC 14 reflex reduces to blurred vision). When consumed it makes the user feel very fast for no mechanical benefit.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Thrown Concoctions/Flash Powder.md

entries.title: Siphoning Salt
entries.content: 600g (DC 16) Mineral deposits containing salt exposed to powerful magic can sometimes cause the salt to take a blue hue, giving it anti-magic properties. Mages are hesitant to mass produce it for obvious reasons. When thrown on a spell with a persistent effect, it acts as dispel magic at CL 3.
entries.path: Equipment and Economics/Equipment Types/Alchemical Items/Thrown Concoctions/Siphoning Salt.md

entries.title: Stink Oil
entries.content: 50g (DC 11) Like alchemist fire, stink oil only requires common chemicals. It also requires a little bit of dead organic matter. Most alchemists use their own dead skin flakes to save time. Throw this vial in the space of a creature to cause it to be sickened 2 for one minute (DC 14 fort negates).
